tEn: AI Revolution: Navigating the Good and the Bad
sEn: Sam discusses the inevitable AI revolution, its potential for abundance, and the need to navigate job displacement and societal change.
sZh: Sam讨论了不可避免的AI革命，其带来富足的潜力，以及应对工作替代和社会变革的必要性。

consensus.en: AI will bring net positive change but with significant societal disruption and loss.
consensus.zh: AI 将带来净正面变化，但伴随重大的社会颠覆和损失。

consensus.en: Scaling AI leads to exponential growth in capabilities, but progress is gradual.
consensus.zh: Scaling（规模扩张）AI 导致能力指数级增长，但进步是渐进的。

consensus.en: AI safety and alignment are critical to prevent catastrophic outcomes.
consensus.zh: AI 安全和对齐对于防止灾难性后果至关重要。

consensus.en: Universal basic income may be needed to cushion job displacement from AI.
consensus.zh: 可能需要全民基本收入来缓冲 AI 导致的失业。

consensus.en: AI will dramatically accelerate scientific discovery and solve global problems.
consensus.zh: AI 将极大加速科学发现并解决全球问题。

contrarian.en: AI will replace creative jobs first, not blue-collar labor as commonly assumed.
contrarian.zh: AI 将首先取代创意工作，而非普遍认为的蓝领劳动。

contrarian.en: Short timelines and slow takeoff for AGI is the safest and most controllable path.
contrarian.zh: AGI（通用人工智能）的短时间线和慢速起飞是最安全、最可控的路径。

contrarian.en: Humans in current form will persist longer than expected, not be replaced soon.
contrarian.zh: 当前形式的人类将比预期更持久，不会很快被取代。

contrarian.en: Corruption is a bigger societal problem than AI risk, and AI can help reduce it.
contrarian.zh: 腐败是比 AI 风险更大的社会问题，而 AI 可以帮助减少腐败。

contrarian.en: Neural interfaces may not be necessary; access to compute power is more transformative.
contrarian.zh: 神经接口可能并非必需；获取算力更具变革性。

transcript.sec: Introduction and general perspective on AI

turns.en: Hello Sam, what's happening?
turns.zh: 你好 Sam，最近怎么样？

turns.en: Not much. Thanks for coming in here, appreciate it. Thanks for having me.
turns.zh: 没什么。谢谢你邀请我来，很感激。谢谢你的招待。

turns.en: So what have you done with AI? I mean, everyone is fascinated by it, absolutely blown away at the current capability and wondering what the potential for the future is, and whether or not that's a good thing.
turns.zh: 那么你在 AI 方面做了什么？我的意思是，每个人都对它着迷，对目前的能力感到震惊，并想知道未来的潜力，以及这是否是一件好事。

turns.en: I think it's going to be a great thing, but I think it's not going to be all a great thing. That's where all the complexity comes in for people. It's not this clean story of we're going to do this and it's all going to be great. It's going to be net great, but it's going to be like a technological revolution, a societal revolution. And those always come with change. Even if it's net wonderful, there are things we're going to lose along the way: some kinds of jobs, some parts of our way of life, some parts of the way we live are going to change or go away. No matter how tremendous the upside is—and I believe it will be tremendously good—there's a lot of stuff we got to navigate through to make sure. That's a complicated thing for anyone to wrap their heads around, and there are deep and super understandable emotions around that.
turns.zh: 我认为这会是一件伟大的事情，但不会全是好事。这就是所有复杂性所在。这不是一个简单的故事：我们做这件事，一切都会很棒。它总体上会很棒，但它将是一场技术革命，一场社会革命。而这些总是伴随着变化。即使总体上很美好，我们也会在这个过程中失去一些东西：某些工作、某些生活方式、某些生活部分会改变或消失。无论好处有多大——我相信它会非常好——我们都需要应对很多事情。这对任何人来说都是一个复杂的问题，而且围绕着它有着深刻且非常可以理解的情感。

turns.en: That's a very honest answer, that it's not all going to be good. But it seems inevitable at this point.
turns.zh: 这是一个非常诚实的回答，并非一切都会好。但在这一点上，它似乎是不可避免的。

turns.en: Yeah, it's definitely inevitable. My view of the world: when you're a kid in school, you learn about this technological revolution and then that one. My view now, looking backward and forward, is that this is one long technological revolution. First we had to figure out agriculture so that we had the resources and time to figure out how to build machines. Then we got the Industrial Revolution, and that made us learn about a lot of other scientific discovery too. It let us do the computer revolution, and that's now letting us, as we scale up to these massive systems, do the AI revolution. But it really is just one long story of humans discovering science and technology and co-evolving with it. I think it's the most exciting story of all time. I think it's how we get to this world of abundance. Although we do have these things to navigate and there will be these downsides, if you think about what it means for the world and for people's quality of life, if we can get to a world where the cost of intelligence dramatically falls and the abundance that comes with that goes way up, I think we'll do the same thing with energy. I think those are the two key inputs to everything else we want. So if we can have abundant and cheap energy and intelligence, that will transform people's lives largely for the better.
turns.zh: 是的，这绝对是不可避免的。我对世界的看法：当你还是个学生时，你了解到这个技术革命和那个技术革命。我现在回顾过去和展望未来的观点是，这是一场漫长的技术革命。首先我们必须发展农业，这样我们才有资源和时间去研究如何制造机器。然后我们有了工业革命，这也让我们学到了很多其他科学发现。它让我们实现了计算机革命，而现在，随着我们扩展到这些庞大的系统，它让我们实现了 AI 革命。但这实际上只是人类发现科学和技术并与之共同进化的一个漫长故事。我认为这是有史以来最激动人心的故事。我认为这是我们走向富足世界的方式。尽管我们确实需要应对这些事情，并且会有这些负面影响，但如果你思考这对世界和人们生活质量意味着什么，如果我们能到达一个智能成本大幅下降、随之而来的富足大幅上升的世界，我认为我们会对能源做同样的事情。我认为这是我们所想要的一切的两个关键输入。所以如果我们能拥有丰富且廉价的能源和智能，那将极大地改善人们的生活。

turns.en: In the same way that if we could go back now 500 years and look at someone's life, we'd say there are some great things but they didn't have this, they didn't have that. Can you believe they didn't have modern medicine? That's what people are going to look back at us like. But in 50 years, when you think about the people that currently really rely on jobs that AI will replace—whether it's truck drivers, automation workers, people that work in factory assembly lines—what strategies can be put in place to mitigate the negative downsides of those jobs being eliminated by AI?
turns.zh: 就像如果我们现在能回到 500 年前，看看某人的生活，我们会说有一些很棒的东西，但他们没有这个，没有那个。你能相信他们没有现代医学吗？这就是人们将来回顾我们时的感觉。但在 50 年后，当你想到那些目前依赖将被 AI 取代的工作的人——无论是卡车司机、自动化工人、工厂流水线工人——可以采取什么策略来减轻这些工作被 AI 淘汰的负面影响？

turns.en: I'll talk about some general thoughts, but I find making very specific predictions difficult because the way the technology goes has been so different than even my own intuitions. Can we maybe stop there and back up a little?
turns.zh: 我会谈一些一般性的想法，但我发现做出非常具体的预测很困难，因为技术的发展方式甚至与我自己的直觉大相径庭。我们能不能先停一下，稍微回顾一下？

turns.en: What were your initial thoughts?
turns.zh: 你最初的想法是什么？

turns.en: If you had asked me 10 years ago, I would have said: first, AI is going to come for blue-collar labor—basically it's going to drive trucks and do factory work, handle heavy machinery. Then maybe after that, it'll do some kinds of cognitive labor, but not the really hard stuff—it won't be proving new mathematical theorems, discovering new science, writing code. And then eventually, maybe last of all, maybe never because human creativity is this magic special thing, last of all it'll come for the creative jobs. That's what I would have said. Now, it looks to me like, for a while, AI is much better at doing tasks than doing jobs. It can do these little pieces super well, but sometimes it goes off the rails; it can't keep very long coherence. So people are instead just able to do their existing jobs way more productively, but you really still need the human there today. And B, it's going exactly the other direction: it could do the creative work first, stuff like coding second, other kinds of cognitive labor third, and we're the furthest away from humanoid robots.
turns.zh: 如果你 10 年前问我，我会说：首先，AI 会取代蓝领劳动——基本上它会开卡车、做工厂工作、操作重型机械。然后在那之后，它会做一些认知劳动，但不会是真正困难的事情——它不会证明新的数学定理、发现新的科学、编写代码。然后最终，也许最后，也许永远不会，因为人类创造力是这种神奇特殊的东西，最后它才会取代创意工作。这就是我过去会说的。现在，在我看来，在一段时间内，AI 更擅长完成任务而不是完成工作。它可以很好地完成这些小部分，但有时会出错；它无法保持很长的连贯性。所以人们反而能够更高效地完成现有工作，但今天你仍然需要人类。而且 B，它正朝着完全相反的方向发展：它可能先做创意工作，然后是编码之类的事情，再然后是其他类型的认知劳动，而我们离人形机器人最远。

turns.en: So back to the initial question: if we do have something that completely eliminates factory workers, completely eliminates truck drivers, delivery drivers, things along those lines, that creates this massive vacuum in our society.
turns.zh: 所以回到最初的问题：如果我们确实有东西完全取代了工厂工人，完全取代了卡车司机、送货司机等等，这会在我们的社会中造成巨大的真空。

turns.en: I think there are things that we're going to do that are good to do but not sufficient. So I think at some point we will do something like a UBI or some other kind of very long-term unemployment insurance, something. We'll have some way of giving people, like redistributing money in society as a cushion for people as they figure out the new jobs. And I maybe should touch on that: I'm not a believer at all that there won't be lots of new jobs. I think human creativity, desire for status, wanting different ways to compete, invent new things, feel part of a community, feel valued—that's not going to go away. People have worried about that forever. What happens is we get better tools and we just invent new things and more amazing things to do. And there's a big universe out there—I mean that literally, in that space is really big, but also there's just so much stuff we can all do. If we do get to this world of abundant intelligence, where you can just think of a new idea and it gets created, but again, that doesn't provide great solutions to people who are losing their jobs today. So saying there's going to be this great indefinite stuff in the future, people are like, 'What are we doing today?' So we'll, as a society, do things like UBI and other ways of redistribution, but I don't think that could be at the core of what people...
turns.zh: 我认为我们会做一些好事，但还不够。所以我认为在某个时候，我们会做一些事情，比如全民基本收入（UBI）或其他某种非常长期的失业保险之类的东西。我们会有某种方式给人们重新分配社会中的金钱，作为人们在寻找新工作时的缓冲。我或许应该提一下：我完全不相信不会有很多新工作。我认为人类的创造力、对地位的渴望、想要以不同方式竞争、发明新事物、成为社区的一部分、感到被重视——这些不会消失。人们一直担心这个。实际情况是，我们得到更好的工具，然后我们发明新事物和更令人惊叹的事情去做。而且外面有一个巨大的宇宙——我字面意思是指太空真的很大，但也有很多我们可以做的事情。如果我们真的到达了这个智能充裕的世界，你可以想到一个新想法，它就会被创造出来，但同样，这并不能为今天失业的人提供很好的解决方案。所以说未来会有这些伟大的无限的东西，人们会问：‘我们今天在做什么？’所以，作为一个社会，我们会做像 UBI 和其他再分配方式的事情，但我不认为这能成为人们核心的……

transcript.sec: Agency and Meaningful Work in an AGI Future

turns.en: I think what people want is agency, self-determination, the ability to play a role in architecting the future along with the rest of society, the ability to express themselves and create something meaningful to them. Also, a lot of people work jobs they hate. As a society, we're always a bit confused about whether we want to work more or less, but somehow we all get to do something meaningful and play our role in driving the future forward. That's really important. I hope that as those long-haul truck driving jobs go away—people have been wrong about predicting how fast that will happen, but it will happen—we figure out not just a way to solve the economic problem by giving people money every month, but also a way to share ownership and decision-making over the future. I often say about AGI that everyone realizes we'll have to share the benefits, but we also have to share decision-making and access to the system itself. I'd be more excited about a world where, rather than giving everyone on Earth one eight-billionth of the AGI money—which we should also do—we say you get one eight-billionth slice of the system. You can sell it to someone else, to a company, pool it with others, use it for any creative pursuit you want, or to figure out how to start a new business. With that, you get a voting right over how it's all used. So the better the AGI gets, the more your little one eight-billionth ownership is worth to you.
turns.zh: 我认为人们想要的是自主权、自我决定权，能够与社会其他成员一起在构建未来中发挥作用，能够表达自己并创造对自己有意义的东西。此外，很多人做着他们讨厌的工作。作为一个社会，我们总是有点困惑，是想要工作更多还是更少，但无论如何，我们都能做一些有意义的事情，并在推动未来发展中扮演自己的角色。这非常重要。我希望随着那些长途卡车驾驶工作的消失——人们一直错误预测这会发生多快，但它终将发生——我们不仅能通过每月给人们钱来解决经济问题，还能找到一种分享所有权和未来决策权的方式。我经常说，关于AGI，每个人都意识到我们必须分享它的好处，但我们也必须分享决策权和系统本身的访问权。我更期待这样一个世界：与其给地球上的每个人八十分之一的AGI资金——我们也应该这样做——不如说每个人得到系统的一小部分。你可以把它卖给其他人、卖给公司、与他人合并、用于任何你想要的创造性追求，或者用来想办法创办新企业。有了这个，你就获得了关于这一切如何使用的投票权。所以AGI变得越好，你那小小的八十分之一所有权就越有价值。

turns.en: We were joking around the other day on the podcast where I was saying that what we need is an AI government, that we should have an AI president and have AI make all the decisions. Something that's completely unbiased, absolutely rational, with the accumulated knowledge of all human history at its disposal, including all knowledge of psychology and psychological studies. But that comes with a host of pitfalls and issues that people have with it.
turns.zh: 前几天我们在播客上开玩笑，我说我们需要一个AI政府，应该有一个AI总统，让AI做所有决定。一个完全无偏见、绝对理性、掌握整个人类历史积累知识的东西，包括所有心理学和心理学研究的知识。但这伴随着一大堆人们担心的陷阱和问题。

turns.en: I think we're still very far away from a system that is capable enough and reliable enough that any of us would want that. But I'll tell you something I love about that someday. Let's say that thing gets built. The fact that it can go around and talk to every person on Earth, understand their exact preferences at a very deep level—how they think about this issue and that one, how they balance trade-offs, what they want—and then understand all that and collectively optimize for the collective preferences of humanity or citizens of the US. That's awesome, as long as it's not co-opted. Our government currently is co-opted. We know for sure that our government is heavily influenced by special interests. If we could have an artificial intelligence government that has no influence on it, nothing has influence on it—what a fascinating idea. It's possible, and I think it might be the only way to get completely objective, the absolute most intelligent decision for virtually every problem and dilemma we face in society.
turns.zh: 我认为我们离一个足够强大和可靠、让我们任何一个人都想要的系统还很远。但我要告诉你，有一天我喜欢它的地方。假设这个东西被造出来了。它可以和地球上的每个人交谈，在非常深的层面上理解他们的确切偏好——他们如何看待这个问题和那个问题，如何权衡取舍，想要什么——然后理解所有这些，并集体优化人类或美国公民的集体偏好。这太棒了，只要它不被收买。我们现在的政府已经被收买了。我们确信我们的政府受到特殊利益的严重影响。如果我们能有一个人工智能政府，没有任何东西能影响它——多么迷人的想法。这是可能的，我认为这可能是获得完全客观、绝对最聪明的决策的唯一途径，几乎可以解决我们社会面临的每一个问题和困境。

turns.en: Would you truly be comfortable handing over final decision-making and saying, 'All right AI, you got it'?
turns.zh: 你真的愿意交出最终决策权，说‘好吧AI，你来决定’吗？

turns.en: No, but I'm not comfortable doing that with anybody. I was uncomfortable with the Patriot Act, I'm uncomfortable with many decisions being made. There's so much obvious evidence that decisions are not being made in the best interest of the overall well-being of the people. They're made in the interest of whatever gigantic corporations that have donated, the military-industrial complex, the pharmaceutical industrial complex. Money has a massive influence on our society and the choices that get made, and the overall good or bad for the population.
turns.zh: 不，但我对任何人这样做都不舒服。我对《爱国者法案》感到不舒服，我对许多正在做出的决定感到不舒服。有太多明显的证据表明，决策并非为了人民的整体福祉。它们是为了那些捐款的巨型公司、军工复合体、制药复合体的利益。金钱对我们的社会和做出的选择，以及对民众的整体好坏，有着巨大的影响。

turns.en: I have no disagreement that the current system is super broken, not working for people, super corrupt, and unbelievably run by money. I think there is a way to do a better job than that with AI, in some way. But this might just be a factor of sitting with the systems all day and watching all the ways they fail. We've got a long way to go.
turns.zh: 我完全同意当前系统非常糟糕，不为人民服务，极其腐败，而且令人难以置信地被金钱操控。我认为AI可以在某种程度上做得更好。但这可能只是整天与系统打交道、观察它们所有失败方式的一个因素。我们还有很长的路要走。

turns.en: When you think of AGI and the possible future, do you ever extrapolate and pause, saying, 'If this thing becomes sentient and has the ability to make better versions of itself, how long before we're literally dealing with a God?'
turns.zh: 当你想到AGI和可能的未来时，你是否曾停下来推断：‘如果这个东西有了意识，并且有能力制造更好的自己，我们离真正面对一个神还有多远？’

turns.en: The way I think about this is that AGI used to be a very binary moment—before and after. I think I was totally wrong about that. The right way to think about it is a continuum of intelligence, a smooth exponential curve back all the way to that sort of smooth curve of technological revolution. The amount of compute power we can put into the system, the scientific ideas about how to make it more efficient and smarter, to give it the ability to do reasoning, to think about how to improve itself—that will all come. But my model for a long time—if you look at the world of AGI thinkers, there are two axes that matter, particularly around safety issues: short timelines vs. long timelines to the first milestone of AGI, and then takeoff speed once we get there—from there to the point where it's capable of rapid self-improvement, is that a slow or fast process? The world I think we're heading into, and also the world that I think is the most controllable and safest, is the short timelines and slow takeoff quadrant. I think we're going to have—there were a lot of very smart people for a while who thought that the thing you just talked about happens in a day or three days. That doesn't seem likely to me given the shape of the technology as we understand it now. Even if that happens in a decade or three decades, it's still the blink of an eye from a historical perspective. There are going to be some real challenges to getting that right, and the decisions we make—the safety systems and the checks—that the world...
turns.zh: 我对这个问题的看法是，AGI曾经是一个非常二元的时刻——之前和之后。我认为我完全错了。正确的思考方式是把它看作一个智能的连续体，一条平滑的指数曲线，一直回溯到那种技术革命的平滑曲线。我们可以投入系统的算力量，关于如何使其更高效、更智能、赋予它推理能力、思考如何自我改进的科学想法——这些都会到来。但长期以来我的模型——如果你看看AGI思想家的世界，有两个轴很重要，特别是在安全问题上：到达第一个AGI里程碑的短时间线或长时间线，然后一旦我们到达那里，起飞速度——从那里到它能够快速自我改进的点，是一个缓慢还是快速的过程？我认为我们正在走向的世界，也是我认为最可控和最安全的世界，是短时间线和慢起飞象限。我认为我们将会有——曾经有很多非常聪明的人认为你刚才说的事情会在一天或三天内发生。鉴于我们现在对技术的理解，这在我看来不太可能。即使这发生在十年或三十年内，从历史的角度来看，仍然是一眨眼的功夫。要把它做对会有一些真正的挑战，我们做出的决定——安全系统和检查——世界……

transcript.secZh: AGI时代的自主与有意义工作

turns.en: It's super important to put in place how we think about global regulation or rules of the road from a safety perspective for those projects. You can imagine many things going horribly wrong, but I feel cheerful about the progress the world is making towards taking this seriously. It reminds me of what I've read about the conversations around the development of nuclear weapons. In terms of public consciousness, this has emerged very rapidly. I don't think anyone was really aware—people were aware of the concept of artificial intelligence, but they didn't think it would be implemented so comprehensively so quickly.
turns.zh: 从安全角度为这些项目制定全球监管或规则至关重要。你可以想象很多可怕的事情发生，但我对世界在认真对待这件事上取得的进展感到乐观。这让我想起我读到的关于核武器发展时的对话。就公众意识而言，这出现得非常迅速。我不认为有人真正意识到——人们知道人工智能的概念，但没想到它会如此全面、如此迅速地实现。

turns.en: ChatGPT is on 4.5 now? 4? 4 and with 4.5 there'll be some sort of exponential increase in its abilities. It'll be somewhat better each step. Humans have this ability to get used to any new technology so quickly. The thing I think was unusual about the launch of ChatGPT 3.5 and then 4 was that people hadn't really been paying attention. That's part of the reason we deploy—we think it's very important that people and institutions have time to gradually understand this, react, and co-design the society we want with it. If you just build AGI in secret in a lab and then drop it on the world all at once, I think that's a really bad idea. We had been trying to talk to the world about this for a while. If you don't give people something they can feel and use in their lives, they don't quite take it seriously. Everybody's busy. There was this big overhang from where the technology was to where public consciousness was. Now that's caught up. I think people understand it. I don't expect the jump from 4 to whenever we finish 4.5 to be crazy. I think the crazy adjustment people have had to go through has mostly happened. Most people have gone from thinking AGI was science fiction and very far off to something that is going to happen. That was a one-time reframe. Now, every year you get a new iPhone. Over 15 years they've gotten dramatically better, but iPhone to iPhone you're like, okay, it's a little better. But if you hold up the first iPhone to the 15, that's a big difference. GPT-3.5 to AGI will be a big difference, but along the way it'll just get incrementally better.
turns.zh: ChatGPT 现在是 4.5 吗？4？4 和 4.5 之间会有某种指数级的能力提升。每一步都会更好一些。人类有一种快速适应任何新技术的能力。我认为 ChatGPT 3.5 和 4 的发布不同寻常的地方在于，人们之前并没有真正关注。这是我们部署的部分原因——我们认为让人们和机构有时间逐渐理解、反应并共同设计我们想要的社会非常重要。如果你秘密地在实验室里建造 AGI，然后一下子扔给世界，我认为那是个非常糟糕的主意。我们一直在试图与世界对话。如果你不给人们一些他们能在生活中感受和使用的东西，他们不会太当真。每个人都很忙。从技术所处的位置到公众意识之间存在巨大的落差。现在这个落差已经弥合了。我认为人们理解了。我不认为从 4 到我们完成 4.5 的跳跃会那么疯狂。我认为人们必须经历的疯狂调整已经基本发生了。大多数人已经从认为 AGI 是科幻小说且非常遥远，转变为认为它即将发生。那是一次性的重新框架。现在，每年你都会得到一部新 iPhone。15 年来它们变得好得多，但 iPhone 到 iPhone 你会觉得，好吧，好了一点。但如果你把第一部 iPhone 和 15 放在一起，那差别就大了。GPT-3.5 到 AGI 将是一个巨大的差异，但一路上它只会逐步改进。

turns.en: Do you think about the convergence of things like Neuralink and other competing technologies that try to implement some sort of connection between the human biological system and technology? Do you want one of those things in your head?
turns.zh: 你是否考虑过 Neuralink 和其他竞争技术的融合，这些技术试图在人类生物系统和技术之间建立某种连接？你想在脑子里装一个那样的东西吗？

turns.en: I don't, until everybody does. I have a joke about it, but the idea is that once it gets going, you kind of have to because everybody's going to have it. One of the hard questions about all the merge stuff is exactly what you just said: as a society, are we going to let some people merge with AGI and not others? If we do, and you choose not to, what does that mean for you? Will you be protected? How do you get that moment right? If we imagine all the way out to the sci-fi future, there have been a lot of sci-fi books written about how you get that moment right—who gets to do that first, what about people who don't want to, how do you make sure the people that do it first actually help lift everybody up together, how do you make sure people who want to just live their very human life get to do that? That stuff is really hard and honestly so far off from my problems of the day that I don't get to think about it as much as I'd like to. But it seems like if we just think logically, that's going to be a huge challenge at some point. People are going to want wildly divergent things, but there is a societal question about fairness and what it means for people who don't do it. Super complicated.
turns.zh: 我不想要，直到每个人都想要。我对此有个玩笑，但想法是，一旦它开始普及，你几乎不得不拥有，因为每个人都会拥有。关于所有融合的事情，一个难题正是你刚才说的：作为一个社会，我们是否会让一些人融合 AGI 而其他人不行？如果我们这样做，而你选择不融合，那对你意味着什么？你会受到保护吗？如何把握好那个时刻？如果我们想象到科幻的未来，有很多科幻书籍写过如何把握好那个时刻——谁先做，不想做的人怎么办，如何确保先做的人真正帮助所有人一起提升，如何确保那些只想过非常人性化生活的人能够做到？这些事情真的很难，而且老实说离我日常的问题太远了，我没有那么多时间去思考。但似乎如果我们只是逻辑地思考，那将在某个时候成为一个巨大的挑战。人们会想要截然不同的东西，但存在一个关于公平以及这对不这样做的人意味着什么的社会问题。非常复杂。

turns.en: On the neural interface side, in the short term, before we figure out how to upload someone's consciousness into a computer—if that's possible at all, and there are plenty of sides you could take on why it's not—the thing I find myself most interested in is what we can do without drilling a hole in someone's head. How much of the inner monologue can we read out with an externally mounted device? If we have an imperfect, low bandwidth, low accuracy neural interface, can people still learn how to use it really well in a way that's quite powerful for what they can now do with a new computing platform? My guess is we figure that out. I'm sure you've seen that headpiece demonstration where someone asks a question, they have the headpiece on, they think the question, and then they literally Google the question and get the answers through their head. That's the kind of direction we've been exploring.
turns.zh: 在神经接口方面，短期内，在我们弄清楚如何将某人的意识上传到计算机之前——如果这完全可能的话，而且有很多理由认为它不可能——我最感兴趣的是我们能在不钻开某人头骨的情况下做什么。我们能通过外部安装的设备读取多少内心独白？如果我们有一个不完美、低带宽、低精度的神经接口，人们是否仍然可以学会很好地使用它，从而对他们用新计算平台能做的事情产生强大影响？我的猜测是，我们会解决这个问题。我肯定你见过那个头戴设备的演示：有人问一个问题，他们戴上头戴设备，思考这个问题，然后他们真的用谷歌搜索这个问题并通过头脑得到答案。这就是我们一直在探索的方向。

turns.en: That seems to me to be step one. That's the Pong of the eventual immersive 3D video games. You're going to get these first steps and they're going to seem sort of crude and slow. It's essentially slower than just asking Siri. I think if someone built a system where you could think words—it doesn't just have to be a question, it could be your passive rambling inner monologue, but certainly could be a question—and that was being fed into GPT-5 or 6, and in your field of vision the words in response were being displayed, that would be the Pong. That's still a very valuable tool to have, and that seems inevitable. There's hard work to get there on the neural interface side, but I believe it will happen.
turns.zh: 在我看来这是第一步。那是最终沉浸式 3D 视频游戏的 Pong。你会得到这些初步步骤，它们看起来有点粗糙和缓慢。它本质上比直接问 Siri 还慢。我认为如果有人构建了一个系统，你可以思考词语——不一定只是问题，也可以是你被动的、漫无边际的内心独白，但当然也可以是问题——然后这些被输入到 GPT-5 或 6，在你的视野中显示回答的词语，那将是 Pong。那仍然是一个非常有价值的工具，而且这似乎是不可避免的。在神经接口方面还有艰苦的工作要做，但我相信它会实现。

turns.en: My concern is that the initial adopters of this will have such a massive advantage over the general population.
turns.zh: 我担心的是，这项技术的早期采用者将比普通人群拥有巨大的优势。

turns.en: That doesn't concern me because that's just like a better computer. You're not jacking your brain into something high-risk. You know what you do when you don't want them? You take off the glasses. That feels fine. This is just the external device.
turns.zh: 这并不让我担心，因为那就像一台更好的电脑。你并没有把你的大脑接入高风险的东西。你知道你不想用的时候怎么办吗？你摘下眼镜。这感觉没问题。这只是外部设备。

turns.en: Do you think we'll be able to communicate with an external device as well, telepathically or semi-telepathically through technology?
turns.zh: 你认为我们也能通过技术与外部设备进行心灵感应或半心灵感应式的交流吗？

turns.en: I do, yeah. I think so too.
turns.zh: 是的，我也这么认为。

turns.en: My real concern is once we take the step to use an actual neural interface, when there's an actual operation.
turns.zh: 我真正担心的是，一旦我们迈出使用实际神经接口的一步，当有实际手术时。

turns.en: And they're using some sort of an implant and then that implant becomes more sophisticated. It's not the iPhone 1 now, it's the iPhone 15. As these things get better and better, we're on the road to cyborgs. We're on the road to like, why would you want to be a biological person? Do you really want to live in a log cabin when you can be in The Matrix? I mean, it seems like we're on this path. We're already a little bit down that path, right? Like if you take away someone's phone and they have to go function in the world today, they're at a disadvantage relative to everybody else. So that's like the lightest weight version of a merge we could imagine. But I think it's worth, if we go back to that thing about the one exponential curve, I think it's worth saying we've lifted off the x-axis already down this path the tiniest bit. And yeah, even if you don't go all the way to a neural interface, VR will get so good that some people just don't want to take it off that much, right? And that's fine for them, as long as we can solve this question of how do we think about what a balance of power means in a world.
turns.zh: 他们正在使用某种植入物，然后那个植入物变得越来越先进。不是 iPhone 1 了，现在是 iPhone 15。随着这些东西越来越好，我们正走在通往半机械人的道路上。我们正走在这样的路上：为什么你想做一个生物人？你真的想住在小木屋里，而你可以活在《黑客帝国》里吗？我的意思是，我们似乎正走在这条路上。我们已经在这条路上走了一点点，对吧？比如如果你拿走一个人的手机，让他们在今天的世界里运作，他们相对于其他人就处于劣势。所以那可能是我们能想象的最轻量级的融合版本。但我认为值得一说的是，如果我们回到那个关于一条指数曲线的事情，我们已经在 x 轴上稍微抬升了一点。而且是的，即使你不完全走到神经接口那一步，VR 也会变得非常好，以至于有些人就是不想摘下来，对吧？这对他们来说没问题，只要我们能解决如何思考世界中的权力平衡这个问题。

turns.en: I think there will be many people. I'm certainly one of them who's like, actually the human body and the human experience is pretty great. That log cabin in the woods is pretty awesome. I don't want to be there all the time. I'd love to go play the great video game, but I'm really happy to get to go there sometimes.
turns.zh: 我认为会有很多人。我肯定是其中之一，觉得实际上人体和人类体验非常棒。树林里的小木屋很 awesome。我不想一直待在那里。我很想去玩那个很棒的视频游戏，但我也很高兴能偶尔去那里。

turns.en: Yeah, there's still human experiences that are just great. Human experience, just laughing with friends, you know, kissing someone that you've never kissed before, that you're on a first date. Those kinds of things are the real moments. Just laughing, having a glass of wine with a friend and just laughing. Not quite the same in VR.
turns.zh: 是的，仍然有一些很棒的人类体验。人类体验，比如和朋友一起大笑，你知道，亲吻一个你从未吻过的人，第一次约会。那些才是真正的时刻。只是和朋友一起喝杯酒，大笑。在 VR 里不太一样。

turns.en: Not now. When VR goes super far, so you can't, you know, it's like you are jacked into your brain and you can't tell what's real and what's not. And then everybody gets super deep on the simulation hypothesis or the like, Eastern religion or whatever. And I don't know what happens at that point.
turns.zh: 现在还不是。当 VR 变得超级先进，以至于你无法分辨，你知道，就像你被接入大脑，分不清什么是真实什么是虚假。然后每个人都深入研究模拟假说或类似东方宗教之类的东西。我不知道那时会发生什么。

turns.en: Do you ever wrestle with simulation theory? Because the real problem is when you combine that with probability theory and you talk to the people that say, well, if you just look at the numbers, the probability that we're already in a simulation is much higher than the probability that we're not. It's never been clear to me what to do about it. It's like, okay, that intellectually makes a lot of sense.
turns.zh: 你有没有纠结过模拟理论？因为真正的问题是当你把它和概率论结合起来，和那些说“如果你只看数字，我们已经处于模拟中的概率远高于我们不在模拟中的概率”的人交谈时。我一直不清楚该怎么做。就像，好吧，这在理智上很有道理。

turns.en: Yeah, I think probably, sure, that seems convincing. But this is my reality, this is my life, and I'm gonna live it. And I've, you know, from 2 AM in my college freshman dorm hallway till now, I've made no more progress on it than that.
turns.zh: 是的，我想可能吧，当然，那似乎很有说服力。但这是我的现实，这是我的生活，我要去活它。而且，你知道，从大学新生宿舍走廊凌晨两点到现在，我在这方面没有取得更多进展。

turns.en: Well, it seems like one of those, there's no, you know, if it is a possibility, if it is real, first of all, once it happens, what are you going to do? I mean, that is the new reality. And in many ways, our new reality is as alien to hunter-gatherers from 15,000 years ago as that would be to us now. I mean, we've already entered into some very bizarre territory where, you know, I was just having a conversation with my kids. We're asking questions about something and I always say, let's guess what percentage of that is this, and then we just Google it and then just ask Siri and we pull it up. Like, look at that. That alone is sorcery compared to how it was when I was a kid, and you had to go to the library and hope that the book was accurate.
turns.zh: 嗯，这似乎是那种，你知道，如果它是一种可能性，如果它是真实的，首先，一旦它发生了，你会怎么做？我的意思是，那就是新的现实。而且在很多方面，我们的新现实对于 15000 年前的狩猎采集者来说，就像那个新现实对我们现在一样陌生。我的意思是，我们已经进入了一些非常奇怪的领域，你知道，我刚刚和我的孩子们聊天。我们问一些关于某件事的问题，我总是说，让我们猜猜那里面有多少比例是这个，然后我们就谷歌一下，然后问 Siri，我们就查到了。就像，看那个。仅此一项，与我小时候相比就是巫术，那时你必须去图书馆，希望那本书是准确的。

turns.en: Totally. I was very annoyed. I was reading about how horrible systems like ChatGPT and Google are from an environmental impact because it's using some extremely tiny amount of energy for each query, and you know how we're all destroying the world. And I was like, before that, people drove to the library. Let's talk about how much carbon they burn to answer this question versus what it takes now. Come on. But that's just people looking for some reason why something's bad. That's not a logical perspective.
turns.zh: 完全同意。我很恼火。我读到关于像 ChatGPT 和 Google 这样的系统对环境的影响有多可怕，因为每次查询只消耗极少的能量，你知道我们都在毁灭世界。我当时想，在那之前，人们开车去图书馆。让我们谈谈他们为了回答这个问题燃烧了多少碳，而现在需要多少。拜托。但那只是人们在找一些理由说某件事不好。那不是逻辑的视角。

turns.en: What we should be looking at is the spectacular changes that are possible through this and all the problems, the insurmountable problems that we have with resources, with the environment, with cleaning up the ocean, climate change. There's so many problems. We need this to solve all of everything else. And that's why we need president AI. If AI could make every scientific discovery but we still had human presidents, do you think we'd be okay?
turns.zh: 我们应该关注的是通过这个可能实现的惊人变化，以及所有的问题，我们在资源、环境、清理海洋、气候变化等方面面临的不可逾越的问题。有这么多问题。我们需要这个来解决所有其他问题。这就是为什么我们需要总统 AI。如果 AI 能做出每一个科学发现，但我们仍然有人类总统，你认为我们会好吗？

turns.en: No, because those creeps would still be pocketing money and they'd have offshore accounts and it would always be a weird thing of corruption and how to mitigate that corruption. Which is also one of the fascinating things about the current state of technology, is that we're so much more aware of corruption. We're so much more, there's so much independent reporting, and we're so much more cogent of the actual problems that are in place. This is really great.
turns.zh: 不会，因为那些混蛋仍然会中饱私囊，他们有离岸账户，腐败以及如何减轻腐败将永远是一个奇怪的问题。这也是当前技术状态的一个迷人之处，我们对腐败的认识更加深刻。我们有更多的独立报道，我们对实际存在的问题更加清晰。这真的很棒。

turns.en: One of the things that I've observed, obviously many other people too, is corruption is such an incredible hindrance to getting anything done in a society, to make it forward progress. And you know, my worldview had been more US-centric when I was younger, and as I've just studied the world more and had to work in more places in the world, it's amazing how much corruption there still is. But the shift to a technologically enabled world, I think, is a major force against it, because everything is, it's harder to hide stuff. And I do think corruption in the world will keep trending down because of its exposure.
turns.zh: 我观察到的一件事，显然很多其他人也观察到，腐败是社会中完成任何事情、取得进展的巨大障碍。你知道，我年轻时世界观更以美国为中心，随着我对世界的研究更多，必须在世界上更多地方工作，令人惊讶的是腐败仍然如此之多。但向技术赋能世界的转变，我认为是对抗腐败的主要力量，因为一切都更难隐藏。而且我确实认为世界上的腐败会因其曝光而持续下降。

turns.en: Yeah, through technology. I mean, it comes at a cost, and I think the loss that, like, I am very worried about how far the surveillance state could go here. But in a world where payments, for example, are no longer like bags of cash but done somehow digitally, and somebody, even if you're using Bitcoin, can like watch those flows, I think that's a corruption-reducing thing.
turns.zh: 是的，通过技术。我的意思是，这是有代价的，而且我认为损失是，我非常担心监控国家能走多远。但在一个支付不再是成袋现金而是以某种方式数字化的世界里，有人，即使你使用比特币，也能观察那些流动，我认为这是减少腐败的事情。

turns.en: I agree. But I'm very worried about central bank digital currency and that being tied to a social credit score. Super against. Yeah, that scares the hell out of me. Super against. And the push to that is not for the overall good of society, that's for control.
turns.zh: 我同意。但我非常担心央行数字货币，以及它与社交信用评分挂钩。超级反对。是的，那把我吓坏了。超级反对。而推动这个不是为了社会的整体利益，而是为了控制。

turns.en: Yeah, I think, I mean, there's many things that I'm disappointed that the US government has done recently, but the war on crypto, which I think is a, like, we can't give this up, like we're going to control this, and all like that, that's a thing that makes me quite sad about the country.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为，我的意思是，有很多事情让我对美国政府的近期作为感到失望，但针对加密货币的战争，我认为那是，我们不能放弃这个，我们要控制这个，诸如此类，那让我对这个国家感到非常难过。

turns.en: It makes me quite sad about the country too. But then you also see with things like FTX, like, oh, this can get really bad without regulation and without someone overseeing it.
turns.zh: 这也让我对这个国家感到非常难过。但你也看到像 FTX 这样的事情，哦，没有监管和没有监督，这可能会变得非常糟糕。

turns.en: Yeah, I'm not anti-regulation. I think there's clearly a role for it. And I also think FTX was a sort of comically bad situation that we shouldn't learn much from.
turns.zh: 是的，我不反监管。我认为它显然有作用。而且我也认为 FTX 是一种滑稽的糟糕情况，我们不应该从中吸取太多教训。

turns.en: Yeah, but it's a fun one. It's totally fun, and I love that story.
turns.zh: 是的，但这是一个有趣的故事。它完全有趣，我喜欢那个故事。

turns.en: I really love the fact that they were all doing drugs and having sex with each other. It's a gripping story because it had everything. They did their taxes with QuickBooks while dealing with billions of dollars. I don't know why I find the word 'polycule' so funny, but that's what they called their relationship—a polyamorous molecule put together. So there were nine of them, and they were polyamorous. That became a meme in Silicon Valley for a while, which I thought was hilarious. You clearly want enough regulation so that can't happen, but I'm not against that happening; I'm against them losing all the depositor money. I mean, polycule is kind of like, go for it. But you want enough regulation so that FTX can't lose all its depositor money. There's an important point here: all this other regulation people talk about didn't keep us safe. The basic thing—like, let's do that—wasn't about all the crypto stuff people were discussing.
turns.zh: 我真的很喜欢他们都在吸毒和互相发生关系这件事。这是一个引人入胜的故事，因为它包罗万象。他们用 QuickBooks 处理税务，同时管理着数十亿美元。我不知道为什么我觉得“polycule”这个词这么好笑，但那就是他们对自己关系的称呼——一个多角恋分子组合。所以有九个人，他们是多角恋。这在硅谷流行过一阵子，我觉得很搞笑。你显然希望有足够的监管来防止那种事发生，但我不反对那种事发生；我反对他们损失所有存款人的钱。我的意思是，polycule 有点像，随他们去吧。但你希望有足够的监管，让 FTX 不能损失所有存款人的钱。这里有一个重要的点：人们谈论的所有其他监管并没有保护我们的安全。基本的事情——比如，我们做那个——并不是关于人们讨论的所有加密货币的东西。

turns.en: The real fascinating crypto is Bitcoin to me. That's the one I think has the most likely possibility of becoming a universal viable currency. It's limited in supply, people mine it with their own resources. That's very fascinating, and I love that it's been implemented. I had Andreas Antonopoulos on the podcast, and he lives it—he spends all his money in Bitcoin, pays his rent in Bitcoin. I helped start a project called Worldcoin a few years ago, so I've learned more about the space. I'm excited about it for the same reasons I'm excited about Bitcoin. The idea of a global currency outside the control of any government is a super logical and important step on the tech tree.
turns.zh: 对我来说，真正迷人的加密货币是比特币。我认为它最有可能成为一种通用的可行货币。它供应有限，人们用自己的资源挖矿。这非常迷人，我喜欢它已经被实现。我邀请过 Andreas Antonopoulos 上播客，他完全生活在其中——他所有的钱都花在比特币上，用比特币付房租。几年前我帮助启动了一个叫 Worldcoin 的项目，所以我对这个领域有了更多了解。我对比特币兴奋的原因同样让我对它兴奋。一个不受任何政府控制的全球货币的想法，在科技树上是一个非常合乎逻辑且重要的一步。

turns.en: Agreed. Why should the government control currency? The government should deal with pressing environmental, social, infrastructure, foreign policy, and economic issues—the things we need to be governed for a peaceful and prosperous society.
turns.zh: 同意。为什么政府要控制货币？政府应该处理紧迫的环境、社会、基础设施、外交政策和经济问题——那些我们需要被治理才能拥有和平繁荣社会的事情。

turns.en: What do you think happens to money and currency after AGI? I've wondered about that. With money going digital, the bottleneck is access. If we get to a point where all information is freely shared everywhere—no secrets, no boundaries, no borders, we're reading minds, complete access to all information—what is money then? Money is this digital thing. How can you possess it if there's no barrier to accessing any information?
turns.zh: 你认为 AGI 之后货币会发生什么？我一直在想这个问题。随着货币数字化，瓶颈在于访问权限。如果我们达到一个所有信息都自由共享的地步——没有秘密，没有界限，没有国界，我们能读心，完全访问所有信息——那货币是什么？货币是这种数字化的东西。如果没有任何信息访问的障碍，你怎么能拥有它？

turns.en: Another way to think about it: money is a way to trade labor or limited hard assets like land and houses. If intellectual labor is readily available and super cheap, that's very different. I think there will always be goods and services we want to be scarce and expensive. Money in that world is a curious idea. It becomes different—it's not a bag of gold in a leather pouch. But then the question is how that money is distributed and how we avoid a horrible Marxist society with a totalitarian government handing it out. My current best idea: if AGI systems become the high-order bits of influence, you need to redistribute not just the money but the access, so people can make their own decisions about how to use and govern it. If you have one idea, you get to do this; if I have another, I get to do that. I have rights to do whatever I want with my part, and if I come up with better ideas, I get rewarded.
turns.zh: 另一种思考方式：货币是交易劳动或有限硬资产（如土地和房屋）的方式。如果智力劳动随时可得且超级便宜，那就会非常不同。我认为总会有我们想要稀缺和昂贵的商品和服务。在那个世界里的货币是一个奇怪的想法。它会变得不同——不再是皮袋里的一袋金子。但问题是如何分配这些货币，以及如何避免一个极权政府发放货币的可怕马克思主义社会。我目前最好的想法：如果 AGI 系统成为影响力的高位比特，你需要重新分配的不只是货币，还有访问权限，这样人们可以自己决定如何使用和治理它。如果你有一个想法，你可以做这个；如果我有另一个想法，我可以做那个。我有权对我的部分做任何我想做的事，如果我想出更好的主意，我会得到奖励。

turns.en: The hardliners against welfare and universal basic income are concerned with human nature. They believe that if you remove incentives and just give people free money, they become addicted and lazy. But isn't that a human biological and psychological problem? Perhaps with AI and some neural interface, we can re-engineer the human biological system and remove problems like jealousy, lust, envy—all those variables that come into play with money and status. If those are eliminated, we become a next version of the human species. We're very far removed from tribal brutal societies of cave people; we all agree this is a better way to live. I was talking about this at my comedy club last night. My wife said everyone came from cave people, which is a sobering thought—that all that is still in our DNA. Those reward systems can be hijacked by just giving people money so they don't have to work or have ambition. That's the fear people have.
turns.zh: 反对福利和全民基本收入的强硬派担心的是人性。他们认为如果你取消激励，直接给人免费的钱，他们会变得上瘾和懒惰。但这难道不是一个人类生物学和心理学的问题吗？也许通过人工智能和某种神经接口，我们可以重新设计人类生物系统，消除嫉妒、欲望、羡慕等问题——所有那些与金钱和地位相关的变量。如果这些被消除，我们就成为人类物种的下一个版本。我们早已远离部落式的野蛮洞穴人社会；我们都同意这是一种更好的生活方式。昨晚我在我的喜剧俱乐部谈到这个。我妻子说每个人都来自洞穴人，这是一个发人深省的想法——所有这些仍然存在于我们的 DNA 中。那些奖励系统可以通过直接给人钱来劫持，这样他们就不必工作或有野心。这就是人们的恐惧。

transcript.sec: Engineering human biology to eliminate conflict

turns.en: Giving people free money but if we can figure out how to literally engineer near the human biological vehicle and remove all those pitfalls if we can enlighten people technologically maybe enlighten is the wrong word but advance the human species to the point where those are no longer dilemmas because those are easily solvable through coding, they're easily solvable through enhancing the human biological system perhaps raising dopamine levels to the point where anger and fear and hate are impossible they don't exist. And if you just had everyone on Molly, how many wars would there be? There would be zero wars. I mean, I think if you could get everyone on Earth to all dally once on the same day, that'd be a tremendous thing, but if you got everybody on Earth to dally every day, that'd be a real loss. But what if they did a low dose of Molly where you just get to ah, where everybody greets people with love and affection and there's no longer concern about competition, instead the concern is about the fascination of innovation and creation, creativity. Man, we could talk the rest of the time about this one topic, it's so interesting.
turns.zh: 给人免费的钱，但如果我们能真正从工程上改造人类生物载体，消除所有那些陷阱，如果我们能在技术上启迪人类——也许‘启迪’这个词不对——但让人类物种进步到那些不再是困境的地步，因为那些问题通过编码就能轻松解决，通过增强人类生物系统就能轻松解决，比如提高多巴胺水平，让愤怒、恐惧和仇恨变得不可能，它们不存在。如果让每个人都嗑Molly，那会有多少战争？零场战争。我的意思是，如果你能让地球上每个人在同一天都嗑一次Molly，那会是件了不起的事，但如果让地球上每个人每天都嗑Molly，那会是真正的损失。但如果他们服用低剂量的Molly，让你达到那种状态，每个人都用爱和情感问候他人，不再关心竞争，而是关心创新和创造的魅力，那会怎样？天哪，我们可以用剩下的时间只聊这一个话题，太有趣了。

turns.en: I think if I could push a button to remove all human striving and conflict, I wouldn't do it. First of all, I think that's a very important part of our story and experience. And also, I think we can see both from our own biological history and from what we know about AI that very simple goal systems—fitness functions, reward models, whatever you want to call it—lead to incredibly impressive results. You know, if the biological imperative is survive and reproduce, look how far that has somehow gotten us as a society. All this stuff we have, all this technology, this building, whatever else, that got here through an extremely simple goal in a very complex environment, leading to all of the richness and complexity of people fulfilling this biological imperative to some degree and wanting to impress each other. So I think evolutionary fitness is a simple and unbelievably powerful idea. Now, could you carefully edit out every individual manifestation of that? Maybe. But I don't want to live in a society of drones where everybody is just sort of on Molly all the time either. That doesn't seem like the right answer. I want us to continue to strive, I want us to continue to push back the frontier and go out and explore. And I actually think something's already gotten a little off track in society about all of that. And we're—I don't know, I think I'm—I thought I'd be older by the time I felt like the old guy complaining about the youth, but I think we've lost something. And I think that we need more striving, maybe more risk-taking, more explorer spirit.
turns.zh: 我觉得如果我能按下一个按钮消除所有人类的奋斗和冲突，我不会这么做。首先，我认为这是我们故事和经历中非常重要的一部分。而且，从我们自身的生物历史以及我们对AI的了解来看，非常简单的目标系统——适应度函数、奖励模型，随便你怎么叫——能带来令人难以置信的成果。你知道，如果生物本能是生存和繁衍，看看它已经把我们社会带到了多远。我们拥有的所有这些，所有技术、建筑等等，都是通过一个极其简单的目标在极其复杂的环境中实现的，导致了人们在一定程度上实现这种生物本能并想互相打动的丰富性和复杂性。所以我认为进化适应度是一个简单而无比强大的概念。现在，你能仔细地编辑掉它的每一个个体表现吗？也许可以。但我也不想生活在一个每个人都整天嗑Molly的无人机社会里。那似乎不是正确答案。我希望我们继续奋斗，继续推进前沿，走出去探索。我实际上认为社会在这方面已经有点偏离轨道了。我们——我不知道，我觉得我——我以为我会在更老的时候才觉得自己是抱怨年轻人的老家伙，但我认为我们已经失去了一些东西。我认为我们需要更多的奋斗，也许更多的冒险，更多的探索精神。

transcript.secZh: 改造人类生物学消除冲突

transcript.sec: Loss of youthful risk-taking and striving

turns.en: What do you mean by you think we've lost something?
turns.zh: 你说我们失去了一些东西，是什么意思？

turns.en: I mean, here's a version of it very much from my own lens. I was a startup investor for a long time, and it often was the case that the very best startup founders were in their early or mid-20s or late 20s maybe even. And now they skew much older. And what I want to know is: in the world today, where are the super great 25-year-old founders? And there are few—it's not fair to say there are none, but there are less than there were before. And I think that's bad for society at all levels. I'm using tech company founders as one example, but like people who go off and create something new, who push on a disagreeable or controversial idea—we need that to drive forward. We need that sort of spirit. We need people to be able to put out ideas and be wrong and not be ostracized from society for it, or not have it be something that they get canceled for or whatever. We need people to be able to take a risk in their career because they believe in some important scientific quest that may not work out or may sound really controversial or bad. Certainly when we started OpenAI and we were saying we think this AGI thing is real and could be done—unlikely but so important if it happens—and all of the older scientists in our field were saying those people are irresponsible, you shouldn't talk about AGI, they're selling a scam, they're being reckless and it's going to lead to an AGI winter. We said we believed, we knew it was unlikely but it was an important quest and we were going to go after it. And the haters—that's important to a society.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，从我的视角来看有一个版本。我长期做创业投资者，过去最优秀的初创公司创始人通常在20岁出头、20多岁中期或甚至20多岁后期。现在他们年龄偏大很多。我想知道的是：在当今世界，那些非常出色的25岁创始人在哪里？很少——说没有是不公平的，但比以前少了。我认为这对社会各个层面都不好。我以科技公司创始人为例，但像那些去创造新事物、推动有争议或不受欢迎想法的人——我们需要他们来推动进步。我们需要那种精神。我们需要人们能够提出想法，犯错，而不会因此被社会排斥，或者被取消等等。我们需要人们能够在职业生涯中冒险，因为他们相信某个重要的科学探索，这个探索可能不会成功，或者听起来非常有争议或糟糕。当然，当我们创办OpenAI时，我们说我们认为AGI是真实的，可能实现——不太可能，但如果发生就非常重要——而我们领域的所有老科学家都说那些人是不负责任的，你不应该谈论AGI，他们在卖骗局，他们鲁莽行事，会导致AGI寒冬。我们说了我们相信，我们知道这不太可能，但这是一次重要的探索，我们要去追求它。而反对者——这对社会很重要。

turns.en: What do you think is the origin? Like, what why do you think there are less young people that are doing those kind of things now as opposed to a decade or two ago?
turns.zh: 你认为根源是什么？为什么现在做这类事情的年轻人比一二十年前少了？

turns.en: I am so interested in that topic. I'm tempted to blame the education system, but I'm sure that interacts with society in all of these strange ways. It's funny, there was this thing all over my Twitter feed recently trying to talk about what caused the drop in testosterone in American men over the last few decades. And no one was like 'this is a symptom not a cause.' Everyone was like 'oh it's the microplastics, it's the birth control pills, it's the whatever.' And I think this is not at all the most important piece of this topic, but it was just interesting to me sociologically that there was only talk about it being about what caused it, not about it being an effect of some sort of change in society.
turns.zh: 我对这个话题非常感兴趣。我倾向于归咎于教育系统，但我确定它与社会以各种奇怪的方式相互作用。有趣的是，最近我的推特上到处都是关于什么导致了过去几十年美国男性睾酮水平下降的讨论。没有人说‘这是症状，不是原因’。每个人都说‘哦，是微塑料，是避孕药，是这个，是那个’。我认为这根本不是这个话题最重要的部分，但对我来说社会学上很有趣的是，只有关于什么导致了它的讨论，而没有关于它是社会某种变化的结果的讨论。

turns.en: But isn't what caused it?
turns.zh: 但难道不是原因吗？

turns.en: Well, there are biological reasons why—when we talk about the phthalates and microplastic pesticides, environmental factors, those are real, totally. And I don't—again, I'm so far out of my depth and expertise here. This was just interesting to me that the only talk was about biological factors and not that somehow society can have some sort of effect on—well, society most certainly has an effect.
turns.zh: 嗯，有生物学原因——当我们谈论邻苯二甲酸盐和微塑料农药、环境因素时，那些是真实的，完全正确。而且我不——再说一次，我在这方面远远超出了我的深度和专业知识。这只是让我觉得有趣的是，唯一的讨论是关于生物因素，而不是社会可以以某种方式产生影响——嗯，社会肯定有影响。

turns.en: Do you know what the answer to this is?
turns.zh: 你知道这个问题的答案吗？

turns.en: I don't. I mean, I've had a—with Dr. Shanna Swan who wrote the book Countdown, and that is all about the introduction of petrochemical products and the correlating drop in testosterone, rise in miscarriages. The fact that these are ubiquitous endocrine disruptors—when they do blood tests on people, they find some insane number, it's like 90 plus percent of people have phthalates in your system. And I appreciate the metal cups. We try to mitigate it as much as possible, but I mean, you're getting it if you're microwaving food, you're getting it, you're just getting it. You're getting it if you eat processed food. You're getting a certain amount of microplastics in your diet, and estimates have been that it's as high as a credit card of microplastics per—in your body, you consume a credit card of that a week. Whoa. The real concern is with mammals, because the introductions—when they've done studies with mammals and—
turns.zh: 我不知道。我的意思是，我和写过《倒计时》一书的Shanna Swan博士有过交流，那本书全是关于石化产品的引入以及与之相关的睾酮下降、流产率上升。这些是普遍存在的内分泌干扰物——当他们给人们做血液检测时，他们发现一些疯狂的数字，比如90%以上的人体内都有邻苯二甲酸盐。我很感激金属杯。我们尽量减轻影响，但我的意思是，如果你用微波炉加热食物，你就会摄入它，你就在摄入。如果你吃加工食品，你也会摄入。你的饮食中会有一定量的微塑料，据估计高达每周一张信用卡的量——你的身体每周消耗一张信用卡的微塑料。哇。真正令人担忧的是哺乳动物，因为引入——当他们用哺乳动物做研究时——

transcript.sec: Feminization of males due to chemicals

turns.en: They've introduced these into their body. There's a correlating one thing that happens is these animals, their taint shrinks. Like the taint of the mammal, when you look at males, it's 50% to 100% larger than the females. With the introduction of these on the males, the taints start shrinking, the penises shrink, the testicles shrink, sperm count shrinks. So we know there's a direct biological connection between these chemicals and how they interact with bodies. So that's a real one. And it's also the amount of petrochemical products we have, the amount of plastics we use. It is such an integral part of our culture, our society, our civilization. It's everywhere. And I've wondered if you think about how these territorial apes evolve into this new advanced species, wouldn't one of the very best ways be to get rid of one of the things that causes the most problems, which is testosterone? We need testosterone. We need aggressive men and protectors. But why do we need them? We need them because there are other aggressive men that are evil, right? So we need protectors from ourselves. We need the good strong people to protect us from the bad strong people. But if we're in the process of integrating with technology, if technology is an inescapable part of our life, if it is everywhere, you're using it, you have the internet of everything that's in your microwave and your television, your computers, everything you use, as time goes on that will be more and more a part of your life. And as these plastics are introduced into the human biological system, you're seeing a feminization of the males of the species. You're seeing a downfall in birth rate. You're seeing all these correlating factors that would sort of lead us to become this more peaceful, less violent, less aggressive, less ego-driven thing, which the world is definitely becoming over time. And I'm all for less violence, obviously. But I don't look... obviously testosterone has many great things to say for it and some bad tendencies too. But I don't think a world, if we leave that out of the equation and just say like a world that has a spirit that we're going to defend ourselves, we're going to find a way to protect ourselves and our tribe and our society into this future, which you can get with lots of other ways, I think that's an important impulse. More than that though, what I meant is about if we go back to the issue of like where are the young founders? Why don't we have more of those? And I don't think it's just the tech startup industry. I think you could say that about young scientists or many other categories. Those are maybe just the ones that I know the best. In a world with any amount of technology, I still think we've got to, it is our destiny in some sense to stay on this curve, and we still need to go figure out what's next, and after the next hill, and after the next hill. And it would be my perception that there is some long-term societal change happening here, and I think it makes us less happy too, right? It may make us less happy. But what I'm saying is, if the human species does integrate with technology, wouldn't a great way to facilitate that be to kind of feminize the primal apes and to sort of downplay the role? Like, should the AGI world... maybe I don't know if it's AGI, I mean maybe it's just an inevitable consequence of technology, because especially the type of technology that we use, which does have so much plastic in it, and then on top of that the technology that's involved in food systems, preservatives, all these different things that we use to make sure that people don't starve to death. We've made incredible strides in that. There are very few people in this country that starve to death. It is not a primary issue. But violence is a primary issue. But our concerns about violence are, and our concerns about testosterone and strong men and powerful people, is only because we need to protect against other same things. Is that really the only reason?
turns.zh: 他们把这种东西引入体内。有一个相关现象是，这些动物的会阴缩小了。比如哺乳动物的会阴，雄性比雌性大50%到100%。雄性引入这些东西后，会阴开始缩小，阴茎缩小，睾丸缩小，精子数量减少。所以我们知道这些化学物质与身体相互作用有直接的生物学联系。这是真实存在的。还有我们使用的石化产品数量，我们使用的塑料数量。它已经成为我们文化、社会、文明如此不可或缺的一部分，无处不在。我在想，如果你考虑这些领地猿如何进化成这个新的高级物种，最好的方法之一难道不是消除最成问题的东西之一——睾酮吗？我们需要睾酮。我们需要有攻击性的男人和保护者。但为什么需要他们？因为还有其他邪恶的攻击性男人，对吧？所以我们需要保护者来保护我们自己。我们需要善良强壮的人来保护我们免受邪恶强壮的人的伤害。但如果我们正在与科技融合，如果科技是我们生活中不可避免的一部分，如果它无处不在，你正在使用它，你的微波炉、电视、电脑，你使用的一切，随着时间的推移，它将越来越成为你生活的一部分。随着这些塑料被引入人类生物系统，你看到雄性物种的女性化。你看到出生率下降。你看到所有这些相关因素，它们会引导我们变得更和平、更少暴力、更少攻击性、更少自我驱动，世界确实在随着时间的推移变得如此。我显然支持减少暴力。但我不认为……显然睾酮有很多好处，也有一些坏倾向。但我不认为一个世界，如果我们把那个排除在外，只是说一个世界有精神，我们要保卫自己，我们要找到保护自己和我们的部落、我们的社会进入未来的方法，这可以通过很多其他方式实现，我认为这是一个重要的冲动。但更重要的是，我指的是回到年轻创始人哪里去了的问题？为什么我们没有更多这样的人？我认为不仅仅是科技创业行业。你可以说年轻科学家或其他许多类别也是如此。这些可能只是我最了解的。在一个有任何科技的世界里，我仍然认为我们必须，在某种意义上这是我们的命运，保持在这个曲线上，我们仍然需要去弄清楚下一步是什么，下一个山丘之后，再下一个山丘之后。我的感觉是，这里正在发生一些长期的社会变化，我认为这也让我们更不快乐，对吧？它可能让我们更不快乐。但我要说的是，如果人类物种确实与科技融合，促进这一点的一个好方法难道不是让原始猿类女性化，并淡化角色吗？比如，AGI世界应该……也许我不知道是不是AGI，我的意思是，也许这只是科技不可避免的后果，特别是因为我们使用的这种科技确实含有大量塑料，再加上涉及食品系统、防腐剂的技术，以及我们用来确保人们不会饿死的所有这些不同东西。我们在这方面取得了巨大进步。这个国家很少有人饿死。这不是主要问题。但暴力是主要问题。但我们对暴力的担忧，以及对睾酮、强壮男人和有权势的人的担忧，仅仅是因为我们需要防范其他同样的事情。这真的是唯一的原因吗？

turns.en: Sure, I mean how many incredibly violent women are out there running gangs? No, that part for sure, clearly not very many. What I meant more is, is that the only reason that society values strong masculinity? Yeah, I think so. I think it's a biological imperative, right? And I think that biological imperative is because we used to have to defend against incoming tribes and predators and animals, and we needed someone who was stronger than most to defend the rest. And that's the concept of the military. That's why Navy SEAL training is so difficult. We want the strongest of the strong to be at the tip of the spear. But that's only because there are people like that out there that are bad. If general artificial general intelligence and the implementation of some sort of a device that changes the biological structure of human beings to the point where that is no longer a concern, like if you are me and I am you and I know this because of technology, violence is impossible. Look, by the time if this goes all the way down the sci-fi path and we're all merged into this one single planetary universal whatever consciousness, then yes, you don't need testosterone. Especially if we can reproduce through other methods. This is the alien hypothesis, right? Why do they look so spindly and without any gender? They have these big heads and tiny bodies. They don't need physical strength. They have some sort of telepathic way of communicating. They probably don't need sounds with their mouths, and they don't need this urge that we have to conquer and to spread our DNA. That's so much of what people do is these reward systems that were established when we were territorial apes. There's a question to me about how much you can ever get rid of that. If you make an AGI and it decides actually we don't need to expand, we don't need more territory, we're just happy, you and me, the whole thing all together, all merges in, we're happy here on Earth. We don't need to get any bigger, we don't need to reproduce, we don't need to grow. We're just going to sit here and run. That sounds like a boring life. I don't agree with that. I don't agree that that would be the logical conclusion. I think the logical conclusion would be they would look for problems and frontiers that are insurmountable to our current existence, like intergalactic communication and trans... what happens when it meets another AGI the other galaxy over? What happens if it meets an AGI that's a million years more advanced? What does that look like?
turns.zh: 当然，我是说有多少极其暴力的女性在经营帮派？没有，那部分肯定，显然不多。我更多的是指，社会重视强男性气质的唯一原因就是这个吗？是的，我认为是这样。我认为这是一种生物学上的必然，对吧？我认为这种生物学上的必然是因为我们过去必须抵御入侵的部落、捕食者和动物，我们需要一个比大多数人更强壮的人来保护其他人。这就是军队的概念。这就是为什么海豹突击队训练如此艰难。我们希望强者中的强者处于矛尖。但这只是因为外面有坏人。如果通用人工智能和某种改变人类生物结构的设备被实施，以至于这不再是问题，比如如果你是我，我是你，我通过科技知道这一点，暴力就不可能了。看，如果这完全走上科幻道路，我们都融合成一个单一的行星宇宙意识，那么是的，你不需要睾酮。特别是如果我们能通过其他方式繁殖。这就是外星人假说，对吧？为什么他们看起来那么细长，没有性别？他们有大脑袋和小身体。他们不需要体力。他们有某种心灵感应交流方式。他们可能不需要用嘴发出声音，也不需要我们那种征服和传播DNA的冲动。人们做的很多事情都是这些奖励系统，这些系统是在我们是领地猿时建立的。对我来说，有一个问题是你到底能摆脱多少。如果你制造了一个AGI，它决定实际上我们不需要扩张，我们不需要更多领土，我们只是快乐，你和我，所有一切融合在一起，我们在地球上很快乐。我们不需要变得更大，不需要繁殖，不需要成长。我们只是坐在这里运行。这听起来像无聊的生活。我不同意。我不同意这会是逻辑结论。我认为逻辑结论是他们会寻找我们当前存在无法克服的问题和前沿，比如星际通信和跨……当它在另一个星系遇到另一个AGI时会发生什么？如果它遇到一个先进一百万年的AGI会发生什么？那会是什么样子？

turns.en: Yeah, that's what I've often wondered. If we are, I call ourselves the biological caterpillars that create the electronic butterfly. We're making a cocoon right now.
turns.zh: 是的，这也是我经常想知道的。如果我们，我称我们自己为创造电子蝴蝶的生物毛毛虫。我们现在正在做一个茧。

transcript.secZh: 化学物质导致男性女性化

turns.en: And we don't even know what we're doing. And I think it's also tied into consumerism. Because what does consumerism do? Consumerism facilitates the creation of newer and better things because you always want the newest, latest, greatest. So you have more advanced technology in automobiles, computers, cell phones, and all of these different things, including medical science. That's all for sure true. The thing I was reflecting on as you were saying that is, I don't think I'm as optimistic that we can or even should overcome our biological base to the degree that I think you think we can. And to even go back one further level, I think society is happiest where there are roles for strong femininity and strong masculinity in the same people and in different people. And I don't think a lot of these deep-seated things are going to be able to get pushed aside very easily and still have a system that works. Sure, we can't really think about what if there were consciousness in a machine someday or whatever what that would be like. And maybe I'm just thinking too small-minded, but I think there is something about us that has worked in a super deep way, and it took evolution a lot of search space to get here, but I wouldn't discount it too easily.
turns.zh: 而且我们甚至不知道自己在做什么。我认为这也与消费主义有关。因为消费主义做了什么？消费主义促进了更新更好的事物的创造，因为你总是想要最新、最棒的东西。所以你在汽车、电脑、手机以及所有这些东西上有了更先进的技术，包括医学科学。这当然都是真的。我在听你说话时反思的是，我不认为我像你那样乐观，认为我们能够或者应该克服我们的生物基础到你认为的程度。再往前推一层，我认为社会最幸福的时候是当同一个人或不同的人身上同时存在强烈的女性和男性特质时。我不认为很多这些根深蒂固的东西能够轻易被推到一边，同时还能保持一个有效的系统。当然，我们无法真正思考如果有一天机器有了意识会是什么样子。也许我只是思维太狭隘了，但我认为我们身上有一些东西在非常深层的层面上起作用，进化花了大量的搜索空间才达到这里，我不会轻易否定它。

turns.en: But don't you think that cave people would probably have those same logical conclusions about life and sedentary lifestyle and sitting in front of a computer and not interacting with each other except through text? Well, I mean, isn't that like what you're saying is correct? How different do you think our motivations are today and what really brings us genuine joy, and how we're wired at some deep level, differently than cave people? Clearly lots of other things have changed, we've got much better tools, but how different do you think he really is?
turns.zh: 但你不觉得穴居人可能也会对生活、久坐不动的生活方式、坐在电脑前、除了文字之外不互相交流得出同样的逻辑结论吗？嗯，我的意思是，这不就是你所说的正确之处吗？你认为我们的动机、真正带给我们真正快乐的东西，以及我们在深层上的连接方式，与穴居人有多大不同？显然很多其他东西变了，我们有更好的工具，但你认为他到底有多不同？

turns.en: I think that's the problem: genetically at the base level there's not much difference, and these reward systems are all we interact with—whether it's ego, lust, passion, fury, anger, jealousy, all these different things. And you think we'll be some people will upload and edit those out? Yes, yeah. I think that our concern with losing this aspect of what it means to be a person, this idea that we should always have conflict and struggle because conflict and struggle is how we facilitate progress, which is true, right? And combating evil is how the good gets greater and stronger if the good wins. But my concern is that that is all predicated on the idea that the biological system that we have right now is correct and optimal. And I think one of the things that we're dealing with with the heightened states of depression and anxiety and the lack of meaning and existential angst that people experience a lot of that is because the biological reality of being a human animal doesn't really integrate that way with this world that we've created. That's for sure, yeah.
turns.zh: 我认为问题就在这里：在基因层面上，基础没有太大区别，这些奖励系统就是我们与之互动的一切——无论是自我、欲望、激情、愤怒、嫉妒，所有这些不同的东西。而你认为有些人会上传并编辑掉这些？是的，没错。我认为我们担心失去作为人的这一方面，这种认为我们应该总是有冲突和斗争的想法，因为冲突和斗争是我们推动进步的方式，这是真的，对吧？与邪恶作斗争是善良变得更伟大、更强大的方式，如果善良赢了的话。但我的担忧是，这一切都基于一个前提，即我们现在的生物系统是正确的和最优的。我认为我们正在处理的抑郁、焦虑、缺乏意义和存在焦虑的高涨状态，很大程度上是因为作为人类动物的生物现实并没有真正与我们创造的这个世界的整合方式相匹配。这确实是肯定的。

turns.en: And I wonder if the solution to that is not to find ways to find meaning with the biological vessel you've been given, but rather to engineer those aspects that are problematic out of the system to create a truly enlightened being. Like one of the things: if you ask someone today what are the odds that in 3 years there will be no war in the world, that's zero. Like nobody thinks no, there's never been a time in human history where we haven't had war. If you had to say what is our number one problem as a species, I would say our number one problem is war. Our number one problem is this idea that it's okay to send massive groups of people who don't know each other to go murder massive groups of people that are somehow opposed because of the government, because of lines in the sand. It's an insane thing. How do you get rid of that? Well, one of the ways you get rid of that is to completely engineer out all the human reward systems that pertain to the acquisition of resources. So what's left at that point? Well, we're a new thing. I think we become a new thing. And what does that thing do? I think that new thing would probably want to interact with other new things that are even more advanced than it. I do believe that scientific curiosity can drive quite that; that can be a great frontier for a long time.
turns.zh: 我想知道解决方案是否不是在你被赋予的生物容器中寻找意义，而是将那些有问题的方面从系统中工程化出来，创造一个真正开明的存在。比如有一件事：如果你今天问别人，三年内世界没有战争的几率是多少，那是零。没有人认为没有，人类历史上从来没有过没有战争的时候。如果你必须说我们作为一个物种的头号问题是什么，我会说我们的头号问题是战争。我们的头号问题是这种观念：派遣大量互不相识的人去谋杀大量因政府、因沙线而对立的人是可以的。这是疯狂的事情。如何摆脱它？嗯，摆脱它的方法之一是完全工程化掉所有与资源获取相关的人类奖励系统。那么那时还剩下什么？嗯，我们是一个新事物。我认为我们变成了一个新事物。那个事物想要做什么？我认为那个新事物可能想要与其他更先进的新事物互动。我确实相信科学好奇心可以驱动这一点；这可以是一个长期的伟大前沿。

turns.en: Yeah, I think it can be a great frontier for a long time as well. I just wonder if what we're seeing with the drop in testosterone because of microplastics, which sort of just snuck up on us—we didn't even know that it was an issue until people started studying how certain is that at this point that that's what's happening? I don't know. It's a very good question. Dr. Shanna Swan believes that it's the primary driving factor of the sort of dropping testosterone and all miscarriage issues and low birth weights. All those things seem to have a direct environmental factor. I'm sure there are other factors too. The drop in testosterone, I mean, it's been shown that you can increase males' testosterone through resistance training and through certain things you can do. Like one of the big ones they found through a study in Japan is cold water immersion before exercise radically increases testosterone. So cold water immersion and then exercise? Yeah, I don't know, I can find that. But it's a fascinating field to study. But I think it has something to do with resilience and resistance and the fact that your body has to combat this external factor that's very extreme that causes the body to go into this state of preservation and the implementation of cold shock proteins and the reduction of inflammation, which also enhances the body's endocrine system. But then on top of that, this imperative that you have to become more resilient to survive this external factor that you've introduced into your life every single day. So there are ways obviously that you can make a human being more robust. We know that we can do that through strength training, and all that stuff actually does raise testosterone. Your diet can raise testosterone, and a poor diet will lower it and will hinder your endocrine system, hinder your ability to produce growth hormone, melatonin, all these different factors. That seems to be something that we can fix, or at least mitigate, with decisions, choices, and effort. But the fact that these petrochemical products—like there's a graph that Dr. Shanna Swan has in her book that shows during the 1950s when they started using petrochemical products in everything—microwave, plastic, Saran wrap, all this different stuff—there's a direct correlation between the implementation and the dip. It all seems to line up. That seems to be a primary factor. Does that have an equivalent impact on estrogen-related hormones? That's a good question.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为它也可以是一个长期的伟大前沿。我只是想知道，我们看到的由于微塑料导致的睾酮下降，这有点悄悄降临到我们身上——我们甚至不知道这是一个问题，直到人们开始研究它有多确定？我不知道。这是一个非常好的问题。Shanna Swan 博士认为它是睾酮下降、所有流产问题和低出生体重的主要驱动因素。所有这些似乎都有一个直接的环境因素。我确定还有其他因素。睾酮下降，我的意思是，已经证明你可以通过抗阻训练和某些你可以做的事情来增加男性的睾酮。比如他们在日本的一项研究中发现的一个大因素是运动前冷水浸泡能大幅提高睾酮。所以冷水浸泡然后运动？是的，我不知道，我可以找到那个。但这是一个迷人的研究领域。但我认为这与韧性和抵抗力有关，以及你的身体必须对抗这个非常极端的外部因素，这导致身体进入这种保存状态，并实施冷休克蛋白和减少炎症，这也增强了身体的内分泌系统。但除此之外，这种你必须变得更有韧性才能在你每天引入生活中的这个外部因素中生存下来的必要性。所以显然有办法让人类更强壮。我们知道我们可以通过力量训练做到这一点，所有这些实际上都会提高睾酮。你的饮食可以提高睾酮，而糟糕的饮食会降低它，并阻碍你的内分泌系统，阻碍你产生生长激素、褪黑激素的能力，所有这些不同的因素。这似乎是我们可以通过决定、选择和努力来修复或至少缓解的事情。但事实上，这些石化产品——比如 Shanna Swan 博士在她的书中有一张图表显示，在 1950 年代，当他们开始在一切中使用石化产品时——微波炉、塑料、保鲜膜，所有这些不同的东西——实施和下降之间存在直接相关性。这一切似乎都吻合。这似乎是一个主要因素。这对雌激素相关激素有同等影响吗？这是个好问题。

turns.en: Actually, I know some of these chemicals they're talking about actually increase estrogen in men. I don't know, but I do know that it increases miscarriages. So I just think it's overall disruptive to humans. Definitely a society-wide disruption of the endocrine system in a short period of time seems like a bad thing and difficult to wrap our heads around. Pollutants and environmental toxins, on top of pesticides, herbicides, and all these other things, and microplastics—there are a lot of factors that are leading our systems to not work well.
turns.zh: 实际上，我知道他们讨论的一些化学物质确实会增加男性雌激素。我不确定，但我知道它会增加流产风险。所以我认为这总体上对人类是有害的。在短时间内对整个社会的内分泌系统造成干扰，这看起来是件坏事，而且很难完全理解。污染物和环境毒素，再加上杀虫剂、除草剂以及其他这些东西，还有微塑料——有很多因素导致我们的系统无法正常运作。

turns.en: But I just really wonder if we are just clinging on to this monkey body. Are we deciding that I like my monkey body? I do too, listen, I love it. But I'm also trying to be very objective. When I objectively look at it in terms of where we are now and all our problems, and look towards the future, one way to mitigate a lot of these would be the implementation of some sort of telepathic technology. You couldn't just text someone or tweet at someone something mean because you would literally feel what they feel when you put that energy out there, and you would be repulsed. And violence—if you were committing violence on someone and you literally felt the reaction of that violence in your own being, you would also have no motivation for violence. If we had no aggressive tendencies, no primal chimpanzee tendencies.
turns.zh: 但我真的想知道，我们是不是只是紧紧抓住这个猴子的身体不放？我们是否决定‘我喜欢我的猴子身体’？我也喜欢，听着，我爱它。但我也努力保持客观。当我客观地审视我们现在的位置和所有问题，并展望未来时，缓解这些问题的一个方法就是实现某种心灵感应技术。你不能只是发条刻薄的短信或推文给别人，因为当你发出那种能量时，你会真切地感受到对方的感受，你会感到厌恶。而暴力——如果你对某人施暴，并且你真切地在自己体内感受到那种暴力的反应，你也会失去暴力的动机。如果我们没有攻击性倾向，没有原始的猩猩倾向。

turns.en: It's true that violence in the world has obviously gone down a lot over the decades, but emotional violence is up a lot. And the internet has been horrible for that. I'm not going to walk over there and punch you because you look like a big strong guy, you're going to punch me back, and also there's a societal convention not to do that. But if I didn't know you, I might send a mean tweet about you and I feel nothing. And clearly that has become a mega epidemic in society that we did not evolve the biological constraints on somehow.
turns.zh: 确实，世界上的暴力在几十年里明显减少了很多，但情感暴力却大幅上升。而互联网在这方面非常糟糕。我不会走过去揍你，因为你看起来是个大块头，你会还手，而且社会规范也不允许那样做。但如果我不认识你，我可能会发一条刻薄的推文，而我毫无感觉。显然，这已经成为社会中的一场大流行病，而我们没有进化出某种生物约束来应对它。

turns.en: Yeah, and I'm actually very worried about how much that's already destabilized us and made us all miserable. It certainly accentuated it, exacerbated all of our problems. I mean, if you read Jonathan Haidt's book 'The Coddling of the American Mind'—have you read it? Great book. It's very damaging to women, particularly young girls growing up. There's a direct correlation between the invention of social media, the introduction of the iPhone, self-harm, suicide, online bullying. People have always talked about people when no one's around, but the fact that they're doing it now openly to harm people is horrible. Obviously, I think it's super damaging to men too, maybe they just talk about it less. But I don't think any of us are set up for this.
turns.zh: 是的，我真的很担心这已经让我们变得不稳定，让所有人都痛苦。它肯定加剧了这一点，恶化了我们所有的问题。我的意思是，如果你读过乔纳森·海特的《美国心灵的娇惯》——你读过吗？很棒的书。它对女性，特别是成长中的年轻女孩伤害很大。社交媒体的发明、iPhone的引入与自残、自杀、网络欺凌之间存在直接关联。人们总是在没人的时候议论别人，但现在他们公开地这样做来伤害别人，这太可怕了。显然，我认为这对男性也伤害很大，也许他们只是说得少一些。但我不认为我们任何一个人是为这个而生的。

turns.en: No, no one is set up for it. And I think famous people know that more than anyone. We all get used to it. You just get numb to it, or if you're wise, you don't engage. I don't engage. I don't even have any apps on my new phone. I decided, okay, nothing that's really smart. No Twitter. I have a separate phone that if I have to post something, I'll pick it up. But all I get on my new phone is text messages.
turns.zh: 不，没有人是为这个而生的。我认为名人比任何人都更清楚这一点。我们都习惯了。你只是变得麻木，或者如果你明智的话，你不去参与。我不参与。我的新手机上甚至没有任何应用。我决定，好吧，没有任何真正智能的东西。没有推特。我有一部单独的手机，如果我必须发点什么，我会拿起它。但我的新手机上只有短信。

turns.en: Is that more just to keep your mind pure and uncluttered?
turns.zh: 这更多是为了保持你的头脑纯净和清晰吗？

turns.en: Yeah, and not tempt myself. And to just—how many times have I gotten up to go to the bathroom first thing in the morning and spent an hour just sitting on the toilet scrolling through Instagram for nothing? It does zero for me. And there's this thought that I'm going to get something out of it.
turns.zh: 是的，而且不让自己受诱惑。只是——有多少次我早上第一件事就是起床上厕所，然后坐在马桶上刷了一个小时的Instagram，什么也没得到？它对我毫无益处。而且还有种想法，以为我会从中得到些什么。

turns.en: I was thinking actually just yesterday about how we've all talked for so long about these algorithmic feeds manipulating us in big ways, and that will happen. But in the small ways already, scrolling Instagram is not even that fulfilling. You finish that hour and you're like, 'I know that was a waste of my time,' but it was over the threshold where you couldn't quite—it's hard to put the phone down. You're just hoping that the next one's going to be interesting. And every now and then, the problem is every 30th or 40th reel that I click on is wild great. I wonder by the way if that's more powerful than if everyone was wild, if everyone was great. It's like the slot machine—you mine for gold, you don't just go out and pick it like daisies. If the algorithm is intentionally feeding you some along the way.
turns.zh: 实际上就在昨天，我在想我们谈论了这么久这些算法推送会以大的方式操纵我们，这会发生。但在小的方面已经如此，刷Instagram甚至没那么有满足感。你刷完一个小时，然后想，‘我知道那是浪费时间’，但它超过了那个门槛，你几乎无法——很难放下手机。你只是希望下一个会有趣。而偶尔，问题是每30或40个短视频中有一个非常精彩。我想知道这是否比每个都精彩更强大。就像老虎机——你挖金子，而不是像摘雏菊一样直接摘。如果算法故意沿途喂给你一些。

turns.en: Yeah, well, there's just a lot out there unfortunately. But in terms of—I was talking to Sha Ali, who's this UFC fighter, obviously has a very strong mind, really interesting guy. One of the things that Shawn said is, 'I get this low-level anxiety from scrolling through things, and I don't know why, what is that?' And I think it's part of—the logical mind realizes it's a massive waste of your resources. I also deleted a bunch of that stuff off my phone because I just didn't have the self-control. I mean, I had the self-control to delete it, but not to stop once I was scrolling through.
turns.zh: 是的，嗯，不幸的是外面有很多东西。但就——我和肖恩·阿里聊过，他是UFC拳手，显然有非常强大的头脑，非常有趣的人。肖恩说的一件事是，‘我刷东西时会有这种低度焦虑，我不知道为什么，那是什么？’我认为这是部分原因——逻辑思维意识到这是对你资源的巨大浪费。我也从手机上删掉了一堆那样的东西，因为我就是没有自控力。我的意思是，我有自控力去删除它，但没有自控力在开始刷之后停下来。

turns.en: Yeah, and so I think we're just getting attention hacked in some ways. There's some good to it too, but we don't yet have the stuff in place—the tools, the societal norms, whatever—to modulate it well. And we're not designed for it. Completely new technology that again hijacks our human reward systems and hijacks all the checks and balances that are in place for communication, which historically has been one-on-one. Historically, communication has been one person to another. When people write letters to each other, it's generally things like love letters or 'I miss you'—they're exposing something they have difficulty expressing in front of you. And it was never—unless the person was a psycho—hateful letters. Whereas the ability to just communicate 'that guy, I hope he gets hit by a bus' is so simple and easy and available. And you don't experience—Twitter seems to be particularly horrible for this. The mechanics work in a way that it really rewards, in ways that I don't think anybody fully understands, tapping into something about human psychology. But that's kind of how you get engagement, how you get followers, how you get the dopamine hits or whatever. And the people who I know that spend all day on Twitter, more of them are unhappy about it than happy.
turns.zh: 是的，所以我认为我们在某些方面被注意力劫持了。它也有一些好处，但我们还没有到位的东西——工具、社会规范等等——来很好地调节它。而且我们不是为它而设计的。全新的技术再次劫持了人类奖励系统，劫持了为沟通而设置的所有制衡机制，而历史上沟通是一对一的。历史上，沟通是一个人对着另一个人。当人们互相写信时，通常是情书或‘我想你’之类的东西——他们暴露了一些难以在你面前表达的东西。而且除非那个人是精神病，否则不会是仇恨信件。而能够简单地传达‘那个家伙，我希望他被车撞’是如此简单、容易和可行。而且你不会体验到——推特在这方面尤其可怕。其机制以某种方式奖励，我认为没有人完全理解，它利用了人类心理的某些东西。但这就是你获得参与度、获得粉丝、获得多巴胺冲击的方式。而我认识的那些整天泡在推特上的人，大多数对此不满而非满意。

turns.en: Oh yeah, they're the most unhappy. I mean, there are quite a few people that I follow that I only follow because they're crazy. And then I'll go and check in on them and see what they're tweeting about. And some of them are on there 8, 10 hours a day. I'll see tweets all day long.
turns.zh: 哦，是的，他们是最不快乐的。我的意思是，我关注了不少人，我关注他们只是因为他们疯了。然后我会去看看他们在推什么。有些人每天在上面待8到10个小时。我会整天看到推文。

turns.en: And I know that person cannot be happy. They're unhappy, and they cannot stop. You can't stop. And it seems like it's their life. It's a... and they get meaning out of it in terms of reinforcement, you know, short-term meaning. I think maybe each day you go to bed feeling like you accomplished something and got your dopamine. And at the end of each decade, you probably like, 'Where'd that decade go?'
turns.zh: 我知道那个人不可能快乐。他们不快乐，而且停不下来。你停不下来。这似乎就是他们的生活。这是一种……他们从中获得意义，从强化中，你知道，短期的意义。我想也许每天你上床睡觉时感觉完成了一些事，得到了多巴胺。而每到十年结束，你可能会想，‘这十年去哪了？’

turns.en: Yeah. I was talking to a friend of mine who was having a real problem with it. He's saying he would be literally walking down the street and he'd have to check his phone to see who's replying, and he wasn't even looking where he was walking. He was just like caught up in the anxiety of these exchanges.
turns.zh: 是的。我和一个朋友聊过，他在这方面真的有问题。他说他走在街上都得看手机，看谁回复了，甚至不看路。他完全被这些交流的焦虑所困。

turns.en: And it's not because of the nice things people say? No, no, no, no. It's all... and with him, he was recognizing that, you know, he was dunking on people and then seeing responses.
turns.zh: 不是因为别人说的好话？不，不，不，不。全是……而且他意识到，你知道，他在贬低别人，然后看回复。

turns.en: Yeah. I stopped doing that a long time ago. I stopped interacting with people on Twitter in a negative way. I just won't do it. Even if I disagree with someone, I'll say something as peacefully as possible. I have like more of an internet troll streak than I would like to admit, and so I try to just not give myself too much the temptation, but I slip up sometimes.
turns.zh: 是的。我很久以前就不那么做了。我不再在推特上以消极方式与人互动。我就是不做了。即使我不同意某人，我也会尽可能平和地说。我其实有比我愿意承认的更严重的网络喷子倾向，所以我尽量不给自己太多诱惑，但有时也会犯错。

turns.en: Yeah, it's so tempting. Totally. It's so tempting, and it's fun. It's fun to just say something shitty. I mean, again, whatever this biological system we were talking about earlier that gets a positive reward, well, moment there's a reaction. You know, there's reactions. You say something outrageous and someone's going to react, and that reaction is like energy. And there's all these other human beings engaging with your idea. But ultimately, it's just not productive for most people, and it's psychologically fraught with peril. There's just so much going on. I don't know anybody who engages all day long that's happy. Certainly not.
turns.zh: 是啊，太诱人了。完全同意。太诱人了，而且很有趣。说点刻薄话很有趣。我是说，再说一次，无论我们之前谈到的这个生物系统，它获得正奖励，嗯，那一刻就有反应。你知道，有反应。你说些离谱的话，就会有人回应，那种回应就像能量。还有那么多其他人在参与你的想法。但最终，对大多数人来说，这没有建设性，而且心理上充满危险。事情太多了。我不认识哪个整天参与其中的人是快乐的。肯定没有。

turns.en: I think I've watched it like destroy—is too strong of a word—but like knock off track the careers or lives or happiness or human relationships of people that are like good, smart, conscientious people that just like got couldn't fight this demon. Because it hacked their dopamine and really accentuated that because people were alone and isolated, and that made it even worse because then they felt even better saying shitty things to people. 'If I'm unhappy, I'm going to say even worse things about you.' And then there was the psychological aspect of it, like the angst that came from being socially isolated and terrified about this invisible disease that's going to kill us all. And so you have this... and then you're interacting with people on Twitter, and then you're caught up in that anxiety, and you're doing it all day. And I know quite a few people, especially comedians, that really lost their minds and lost the respect of their peers by doing that.
turns.zh: 我觉得我见过它像摧毁——这个词太重了——但像让那些善良、聪明、有责任心的人偏离轨道，毁了事业、生活、幸福或人际关系，他们就是无法对抗这个恶魔。因为它劫持了他们的多巴胺，而且因为人们孤独隔离，情况更糟，因为他们觉得对别人说刻薄话感觉更好。‘如果我不快乐，我就说更糟的话。’还有心理层面，比如社交隔离带来的焦虑，以及对这种会杀死所有人的无形疾病的恐惧。所以你有这个……然后你在推特上与人互动，然后陷入那种焦虑，整天都在做。我认识不少人，尤其是喜剧演员，真的因此失去理智，也失去了同行的尊重。

turns.en: I have a lot of sympathy for people who lost their minds during COVID, because what an unnatural thing for us all to go through. And the isolation was just brutal. But a lot of people did, and I don't think the internet, and particularly the social dynamics of things like Twitter, brought out anyone's best.
turns.zh: 我非常同情那些在疫情期间失去理智的人，因为这对我们所有人来说是多么不自然的事。隔离太残酷了。但很多人确实如此，我不认为互联网，尤其是像推特这样的社交动态，能激发出任何人最好的一面。

turns.en: No. Well, I mean, some people, I think if they're not inclined to be shitty to people, I think some people did seek comfort and they did interact with people in positive ways. I see plenty of positive. I think the thing is that the negative interactions are so much more impactful.
turns.zh: 不。嗯，我的意思是，有些人，如果他们没有刻薄倾向，我认为有些人确实寻求安慰，并以积极方式与人互动。我看到了很多积极面。但问题是负面互动的影响要大得多。

turns.en: Yeah. I look, I think there are a lot of people who use these systems for wonderful things. I didn't mean to imply that's not the case. But that's not what drives people's emotions after getting off the platform at the end of the day, right?
turns.zh: 是的。你看，我认为有很多人用这些系统做美好的事。我不是说不是这样。但这不是人们一天结束后离开平台时的情绪驱动力，对吧？
