turns.en: Right. And it's also probably not... if you looked at a pie chart of the amount of interactions on Twitter, I would say a lot of them are on people being angry about things.
turns.zh: 对。而且很可能不是……如果你看推特互动量的饼图，我会说很多都是关于人们对事情生气。

turns.en: How many of the people that you know that use Twitter those eight or ten hours a day are just saying wonderful things about other people all day versus the virulent? Very few. Yeah, very few. I don't know any of them.
turns.zh: 你认识的那些每天用推特八到十个小时的人，有多少整天都在说别人的好话，而不是恶毒的话？很少。是的，很少。我一个都不认识。

turns.en: But then again, I wonder with the implementation of some new technology that makes communication a very different thing than what we're currently doing. What we're doing now with communication is less immersive than communicating one-on-one. You and I are talking, we're looking into each other's eyes, we're getting social cues, we're smiling at each other, we're laughing. It's a very natural way to talk. I wonder if through the implementation of technology, if it becomes even more immersive than a one-on-one conversation, even more interactive, and you will understand even more about the way a person feels about what you say, about that person's memory, that person's life, that person's history, their education, how it comes out of their mind, how their mind interacts with your mind, and you see them, you really see them. I wonder if what we're experiencing now is just like the first time people invented guns, they just started shooting at things, you know?
turns.zh: 但话说回来，我想知道随着新技术的实现，是否会让沟通变得与我们目前所做的截然不同。我们现在做的沟通不如一对一交流那么沉浸。你我在交谈，我们看着对方的眼睛，接收社交信号，互相微笑，大笑。这是一种非常自然的交谈方式。我想知道通过技术的实现，它是否会变得比一对一对话更沉浸、更互动，你会更了解对方对你所说的话的感受，了解那个人的记忆、生活、历史、教育，了解他们的思想如何运作，他们的思想如何与你的思想互动，你看到他们，真正看到他们。我想知道我们现在经历的，是不是就像人们第一次发明枪时，他们只是开始朝东西射击，你知道吗？

turns.en: Yeah. If you can like feel what I feel when you say something mean to me or nice to me, right? That's clearly going to change what you decide to say.
turns.zh: 是的。如果你能感受到我对你说刻薄话或好话时的感受，对吧？那显然会改变你决定说什么。

turns.en: Yes, yeah, yeah. Unless you're a psycho. Unless you're a psycho. And then what causes someone to be a psycho, and can that be engineered out? Imagine a world where we can find the root cause of those things, and through coding and some sort of implementation of technology that elevates dopamine and serotonin and does some things to people that eliminates all of those problems, and allows people to communicate in a very pure way.
turns.zh: 是的，是的，是的。除非你是精神病。除非你是精神病。那么是什么导致一个人成为精神病，这能被设计掉吗？想象一个世界，我们能找到这些问题的根源，通过编码和某种技术的实现，提升多巴胺和血清素，对人做一些事，消除所有这些问题，让人们以非常纯粹的方式沟通。

turns.en: It sounds great. It sounds great. But you're not going to have any rock and roll. You'll stand-up comedy will die. You'll have no violent movies, you know. There's a lot of things that are going to go out the window. But maybe that is also part of the process of our evolution to the next stage of existence. I feel genuinely confused on this.
turns.zh: 听起来很棒。听起来很棒。但你就不会有摇滚乐了。脱口秀会死。你不会有暴力电影，你知道。很多东西都会消失。但也许这也是我们进化到下一阶段存在的一部分。我对此真的感到困惑。

turns.en: Well, I think you should be. I mean, to be sure, how it's going, that's insane. But I don't even have like... beyond belief. I mean, you just... from the... when did OpenAI, when did you first start this project?
turns.zh: 嗯，我觉得你应该困惑。我是说，可以肯定，事情的发展是疯狂的。但我甚至没有……难以置信。我是说，你只是……从……OpenAI是什么时候，你最初是什么时候开始这个项目的？

turns.en: Uh, all the very beginning, end of 2015, early 2016.
turns.zh: 呃，从一开始，2015年底，2016年初。

turns.en: And when you initially started this project, what kind of timeline did you have in mind, and has it stayed on that timeline, or is it just wildly out of control?
turns.zh: 当你最初开始这个项目时，你心里有什么时间线？它是否按那个时间线进行，还是完全失控了？

turns.en: I remember talking with John Schulman, one of our co-founders, early on, and he was like, 'Yeah, I think it's gonna be about a 15-year project.' And I was like, 'Yeah, it sounds about right to me.' And I've always sort of thought since then. Now I no longer think of AGI as quite the end point, but to get to the point where we like accomplish the thing...
turns.zh: 我记得早期和联合创始人约翰·舒尔曼聊过，他说，‘是的，我觉得这大概是一个15年的项目。’我说，‘嗯，听起来差不多。’从那以后我一直这么想。现在我不再把AGI视为终点，但要达到我们完成目标的那一点……

transcript.sec: Timeline and societal impact of AGI

turns.en: We set out to accomplish something that would take us to like 2030, 2031. That has felt to me like a reasonable estimate with huge error bars, and I kind of think we're on the trajectory I would have assumed. What did you think the impact on society would be? When you first started doing this, you said okay, if we are successful and create some massively advanced AGI, what is the implementation and impact on society? Did you sit there with a graph with pros on one side and cons on the other, or just abstractly consider?
turns.zh: 我们当初设定的目标大概要到2030、2031年才能实现。对我来说，这始终是一个误差范围很大的合理估计，而且我觉得我们正沿着我原本假设的轨迹前进。你当时认为对社会的影响会是什么？当你刚开始做这件事时，你说好吧，如果我们成功了，创造出某种极其先进的AGI，那么实施过程和对社会的影响会怎样？你是坐在那里，一边列优点一边列缺点，还是只是抽象地思考？

turns.en: We definitely talked a lot about the cons. Many of us were super worried about, and still are, safety and alignment. If we build these systems, we can all see the great future that's easy to imagine, but if something goes horribly wrong, it's really horribly wrong. So there was a lot of discussion about that, and a big part of the founding spirit is how we're going to solve this safety problem, what that even means. One thing we believe is that the greatest minds in the world cannot solve that in a vacuum; you have to have contact with reality, see where the technology goes. Practice plays out in a stranger way than theory, and that's certainly proven true for us. But we had a very intense list of cons, because there's all the last decades of sci-fi telling you about how this goes wrong and why it's supposed to shoot me right now. I'm sure you've seen the John Connor ChatGPT?
turns.zh: 我们确实讨论了很多缺点。我们中的许多人都非常担心，现在仍然担心，安全和对齐问题。如果我们构建这些系统，我们都能看到容易想象的伟大未来，但如果出了大问题，那真的是大问题。所以有很多讨论，而且创始精神的一个重要部分就是我们如何解决这个安全问题，这到底意味着什么。我们相信的一件事是，世界上最伟大的头脑无法在真空中解决这个问题；你必须接触现实，看看技术走向。实践比理论更离奇，这对我们来说确实如此。但我们有一个非常强烈的缺点清单，因为过去几十年的科幻小说都在告诉你这会如何出错，以及为什么它现在就应该朝我开枪。我肯定你见过约翰·康纳的ChatGPT？

turns.en: I haven't. What is it?
turns.zh: 我没见过。那是什么？

turns.en: It's like John Connor from Terminator, the kid looking at you when you open up ChatGPT. So that stuff was very clear in our minds. Now I think we understand there's a lot of work to do, but we understand more about how to make AI safe. The term 'AI safety' gets overloaded—does it mean don't say something people find offensive, or does it mean don't destroy all of humanity? There's a continuum. But for the 'don't destroy all of humanity' version, we have a lot of work to do, but I think we finally have more ideas about what can work. Given the way the systems are going, we have a lot more opportunities available to solve it than I thought we would have, given the direction we initially thought the technology was going. So that's good. On the positive side, the thing I was most excited about then and remain most excited about now is: what if this system can dramatically increase the rate of scientific knowledge in society? I think all real sustainable economic growth and progress comes from increased scientific and technological capacity. So we can solve all the problems. If AI can help us do that, that's always been the thing I'm most excited about.
turns.zh: 就像《终结者》里的约翰·康纳，那个孩子在你打开ChatGPT时看着你。所以那些东西在我们脑海中非常清晰。现在我认为我们有很多工作要做，但我们更了解如何让AI安全了。'AI安全'这个词被过度使用——它是指不说冒犯性的话，还是指不毁灭全人类？这是一个连续体。但对于'不毁灭全人类'这个版本，我们有很多工作要做，但我认为我们终于有了更多关于什么可行的想法。鉴于系统的发展方向，我们比最初认为的技术方向有更多机会来解决这个问题。所以这很好。在积极方面，我当时最兴奋、现在仍然最兴奋的是：如果这个系统能大幅提高社会中的科学知识率呢？我认为所有真正的可持续经济增长和进步都来自于科学和技术能力的提高。这样我们就能解决所有问题。如果AI能帮助我们做到这一点，那一直是我最兴奋的事情。

turns.en: It certainly seems like that is the greatest potential positive potential of AI—to solve a lot of the problems that human beings have had forever, a lot of the societal problems. That's why I was talking about an AI president. I'm kind of not joking, because I feel like if something was hyper-intelligent and aware of all the variables with no human bias and no incentives other than the greater good for the community of the United States and its interaction with the rest of the world, the elimination of these dictators—whether elected or non-elected—who impose their will on the population because they have a vested interest in protecting special interest groups and industry...
turns.zh: 这看起来确实是AI最大的潜在积极潜力——解决人类一直以来的许多问题，许多社会问题。这就是为什么我在谈论AI总统。我有点不是在开玩笑，因为我觉得如果某个东西超级智能，意识到所有变量，没有人类偏见，没有其他动机，只有美国社区及其与世界其他地区互动的更大利益，消除那些独裁者——无论当选还是非当选——他们将自己的意志强加于民众，因为他们有保护特殊利益集团和行业的既得利益……

turns.en: The thing I find scary when you say that is it feels like humanity not in control, and I reflexively don't like that. But if it's instead the collective will of humanity being expressed without mistranslation and corrupting influences along the way, then yes, I can see it. Is that possible?
turns.zh: 当你这么说时，我觉得可怕的是感觉人类失去了控制，我本能地不喜欢那样。但如果相反，它是人类集体意志的表达，没有沿途的误译和腐败影响，那么是的，我能看到。这可能吗？

turns.en: It seems like it would be. It seems like if it was programmed to do the greater good for humanity and take into account the values and needs of humanity...
turns.zh: 看起来是可能的。看起来如果它被编程为为人类谋取更大利益，并考虑人类的价值观和需求……

turns.en: There's something about the phrase 'do the greater good for humanity' that terrifies me. Very Orwellian. All of it is. But so is artificial general intelligence, for sure.
turns.zh: '为人类谋取更大利益'这个说法让我感到恐惧。非常奥威尔式。所有这些都是。但AGI当然也是。

turns.en: For sure. Open the door, house. I wish I had worked on something less morally fraught. But it's really exciting. I can't imagine a cooler thing to work on. I feel like the luckiest person on Earth. But it's not on easy mode. This is not life on easy mode.
turns.zh: 当然。开门吧，房子。我希望我从事的是道德负担较小的事情。但这真的很令人兴奋。我想象不出更酷的事情可做了。我觉得自己是地球上最幸运的人。但这不是简单模式。这不是简单模式的生活。

turns.en: No, no, no, no.
turns.zh: 不，不，不，不。

turns.en: I mean, you are at the forefront of one of the most spectacular changes in human history. I would say more spectacular than the implementation of the internet. The implementation of the internet was the first baby steps of this. Artificial general intelligence is the internet on steroids, the internet in hyperspace.
turns.zh: 我的意思是，你正处于人类历史上最壮观的变化之一的前沿。我会说比互联网的实施更壮观。互联网的实施是这的第一步。AGI是打了类固醇的互联网，是超空间中的互联网。

turns.en: What I would say is it's the next step, and there will be more steps after, but it's our most exciting step yet.
turns.zh: 我想说的是，这是下一步，之后还会有更多步，但这是我们迄今为止最激动人心的一步。

turns.en: My wonder is what are those next steps after? Isn't that so exciting to think about?
turns.zh: 我的好奇是那之后的下一步是什么？想想是不是很令人兴奋？

turns.en: It's very exciting. I think we're the last people, I really do. I think we're the last of the biological people with all the biological problems.
turns.zh: 非常令人兴奋。我认为我们是最后一批人，我真的这么认为。我认为我们是最后一批带有所有生物问题的生物人。

turns.en: You feel excited about that?
turns.zh: 你对此感到兴奋吗？

turns.en: I just think that's just what it is. You're just fine with it. It is what it is. I don't think you can control it at this point, other than some massive natural disaster that resets us back to the Stone Age, which is also something we should be very concerned with because it seems like that happens a lot. We're not aware of it because the timeline of a human body is so small. The timeline of a human existence is a hundred years if you're lucky, but the timeline of the earth is billions of years. If you look at how many times life on Earth has been reset by comets slamming into the Earth and completely eliminating all technological advancement, it seems like it's happened multiple times in recorded history. I always think we don't think about that quite enough.
turns.zh: 我只是觉得事情就是这样。你对此很坦然。它就是那样。我认为在这一点上你无法控制它，除非发生某种巨大的自然灾害，把我们重置回石器时代，这也是我们应该非常关注的，因为这种情况似乎经常发生。我们没有意识到，因为人类身体的时间线太短了。人类存在的时间线，幸运的话是一百年，但地球的时间线是数十亿年。如果你看看地球上的生命被彗星撞击地球并完全消除所有技术进步重置了多少次，似乎在有记录的历史中已经发生过多次。我总觉得我们对此考虑得不够。

turns.en: We talked about the simulation hypothesis earlier. It's had this big resurgence in the tech industry recently. One of the new takes on it, as we get closer to AGI, is that if ancestors were simulating us, the time they'd want to simulate again and again is right up to the lead-up to the creation of AGI.
turns.zh: 我们之前讨论过模拟假说。最近它在科技行业重新兴起。随着我们越来越接近AGI，一个新的观点是，如果祖先在模拟我们，他们想一遍又一遍模拟的时间点正是创造AGI的前夕。

transcript.sec: Living in the most exciting and problematic time

turns.en: Yeah, so it seems very crazy we're living through this time, but it's not a coincidence at all. This is the time after we had enough cell phones out in the world recording tons of video to train the video model of the world, all being jacked into us via brain implants or whatever, and before everything goes really crazy with AGI. It's also this interesting time to simulate: can we get through? Does the asteroid come right before we get there for dramatic tension? Do we figure out how to make this safe? Do we figure out how to societally agree on it? So that's led to a lot more people believing in it than before.
turns.zh: 是啊，我们活在这个时代看起来非常疯狂，但这绝非巧合。这个时代是在我们有足够多的手机遍布世界、录制海量视频来训练世界视频模型之后，这一切通过脑机接口或其他方式接入我们，而在AGI真正疯狂之前。这也是一个有趣的模拟时刻：我们能挺过去吗？小行星会不会在我们到达之前就撞上来制造戏剧性紧张？我们能找到让这一切安全的方法吗？我们能达成社会共识吗？所以这让比以前多得多的人相信了这一点。

turns.en: Yeah, for sure. And I think this is just where it's going. I mean, I don't know if that's a good thing or a bad thing, it's just a thing. But it's certainly better to live now. I would not want to live in the 1800s and be in a covered wagon trying to make my way across the country.
turns.zh: 没错，我同意。而且我认为这就是大势所趋。我不知道这是好事还是坏事，它就是一件事。但活在现在肯定更好。我可不想活在19世纪，坐着一辆篷车试图穿越全国。

turns.en: Yeah, we got the most exciting time in history yet. It's the best, it's the best. But it's also has the most problems, the most social problems, the most awareness of social, environmental, infrastructure issues that we get to go solve.
turns.zh: 是啊，我们迎来了历史上最激动人心的时代。这是最好的，最好的。但同时也是问题最多的时代，社会问题最多，对社会、环境、基础设施问题的意识最强，而这些问题正等着我们去解决。

transcript.secZh: 生活在最激动与最问题时代

turns.en: Um, and I intuitively think I feel something somewhat different than you, which is I think humans in something close to this form are going to be around for a lot longer. I don't think we're the last humans. How long do you think we have?
turns.zh: 嗯，我直觉上感觉和你有几分不同，我认为接近现在这种形态的人类还会存在很久。我不认为我们是最后的人类。你觉得我们还有多久？

turns.en: Um, longer than a time frame I can reason about, really. Now, there may be a world where some people decide to merge and go off exploring the universe with AI, and there's a big universe out there. But can I really imagine a world where, short of a natural disaster, there are not humans pretty similar to humans from today on Earth doing humanlike things, and the sort of spirit of humanity merged into these other things that are out there doing their thing in the universe? It's very hard for me to actually see that happening. And maybe that means I'm going to turn out to be a dinosaur and be horribly wrong in this prediction, but I would say I feel it more over time as we make progress with AI, not less.
turns.zh: 嗯，比我能够推理的时间框架还要长。现在，也许会有一些人选择融合，和AI一起去探索宇宙，宇宙很大。但我真的能想象一个世界，在没有自然灾害的情况下，地球上没有和今天人类非常相似的人在做人类的事情，而人类的精神融合到那些在宇宙中做自己事情的其他东西里？我很难真正看到这种情况发生。也许这意味着我会变成老古董，这个预测大错特错，但我要说，随着我们在AI上取得进展，我越来越这么觉得，而不是相反。

turns.en: Yeah, I don't feel that at all. I feel like we're done in like a few years. No, a few decades maybe. A generation or two. It'll probably be a gradual change, like wearing of clothes. You know, I don't think everybody wore clothes and they invented clothes. I think it probably took a while. When someone figured out shoes, I think that probably took a while. When they figured out structures, doors, houses, cities, agriculture, all those things were slowly implemented over time and then become everywhere. And I think this is far more transformative and is part of that.
turns.zh: 是啊，我完全没有这种感觉。我觉得我们几年内就结束了。不，也许几十年。一两代人。这可能会是一个渐进的变化，就像穿衣服一样。你知道，我不认为所有人都穿衣服，然后他们发明了衣服。我想这大概花了一段时间。当有人发明了鞋子，我想那也花了一段时间。当他们发明了结构、门、房子、城市、农业，所有这些事情都是随着时间的推移慢慢实现的，然后变得无处不在。而我认为这更具变革性，也是其中的一部分。

turns.en: Because you don't think there will be an option for some people not to merge, right? Just like there's not an option for some people to not have telephones anymore. See, I used to have friends like 'I don't even have email.' Those people don't exist anymore. They all have email. Everyone has a phone, at least a flip phone. I know some people that just can't handle social media and all that jazz, they went to a flip phone. Good for them. I don't know if this is true or not, I've heard you can't walk into an AT&T store anymore and still buy a flip phone. I heard that just changed. You can? Oh really? Someone told me this but I don't know if it's true.
turns.zh: 因为你认为有些人不会有选择不融合的权利，对吧？就像有些人不再有选择不用电话的权利一样。你看，我以前有朋友说‘我连电子邮件都没有’。那些人现在不存在了。他们都有电子邮件。每个人都有手机，至少是翻盖手机。我认识一些人，他们就是应付不了社交媒体之类的东西，就换成了翻盖手机。对他们有好处。我不知道这是不是真的，我听说你不能再走进AT&T商店买翻盖手机了。我听说刚刚变了。可以吗？哦真的吗？有人告诉我的，但我不确定是不是真的。

turns.en: Verizon still has them. I was just there. They still have flip phones. I was like, I like it. I like this little thing that you just call people. And I always romanticized about going to that, but my step was to go to a phone that has nothing on it but text messages. And that's been a few days, feeling good so far.
turns.zh: Verizon还有。我刚去过。他们还有翻盖手机。我当时想，我喜欢这个。我喜欢这个小东西，只能打电话。我一直很向往那样做，但我的步骤是换一个只有短信功能的手机。已经用了几天，目前感觉不错。

turns.en: Yeah, it's good. You know, I still have my other phone that I use for social media, but when I pick that up, I start scrolling through YouTube and watching videos and scrolling through TikTok or Instagram. And I don't have TikTok, but I've tried Threads for a little while, but I'm like, oh, this ghost town, so went right back to X.
turns.zh: 是啊，挺好的。你知道，我还有一个手机用来刷社交媒体，但当我拿起它，我就开始刷YouTube、看视频、刷TikTok或Instagram。我没有TikTok，但我试过Threads一小段时间，但我觉得，哦，这鬼城，所以又回到了X。

turns.en: I live on a ranch during the weekends, and there's Wi-Fi in the house, but there's no cell phone coverage anywhere else. And it's every week I forget how nice it is and what a change it is to go for a walk with no cell phone coverage. It's good for your mind. For some, it's unbelievable for your mind. And I think we have so quickly lost something. 'Out of service' just doesn't happen. It doesn't even happen on airplanes anymore. But that hour where your phone just cannot buzz, no text message either, nothing. I think that's a really healthy thing.
turns.zh: 我周末住在一个牧场，房子里有Wi-Fi，但其他地方都没有手机信号。每周我都忘记这有多好，没有手机信号散步是多么大的改变。这对你的大脑有好处。对有些人来说，对你的大脑好得难以置信。而且我认为我们这么快就失去了某种东西。‘不在服务区’根本不会发生。甚至在飞机上也不再发生了。但那个你的手机无法嗡嗡响、没有短信、什么都没有的时刻，我认为那是一件非常健康的事情。

turns.en: I dropped my phone once when I was in LA. I think it was the last time I dropped the phone. The phone was like, we're done, and it just started calling people randomly. It would just call people, and I'd hang it up, and it would call another person. I'd hang it up. I was showing my wife, I was like, look at this, this is crazy, it's just calling people. So the phone was broken, and I had to order a phone. We were on vacation for like eight days, and it took three days for Apple to get me a phone. I bet you had a great three days.
turns.zh: 有一次我在洛杉矶摔了手机。我想那是我最后一次摔手机。手机就像‘我们完了’，然后它开始随机给人打电话。它就会打给这个人，我挂断，它又打给另一个人。我挂断。我给我妻子看，我说，看这个，太疯狂了，它一直在打电话。所以手机坏了，我得订一个新手机。我们度假大概八天，苹果花了三天才给我送来手机。我打赌你那三天过得很棒。

turns.en: It was amazing. It was amazing because when I was hanging out with my family, I was totally present. There was no options, and I wasn't thinking about checking my phone because it didn't exist. I didn't have one. And there was an alleviation of that low level of anxiety, this sort of like that you have when you always want to check your phone.
turns.zh: 太棒了。太棒了，因为当我和家人在一起时，我完全投入。没有选择，我也不想着看手机，因为它不存在。我没有手机。那种低水平的焦虑得到了缓解，就是那种你总想查看手机时的焦虑。

turns.en: Yeah, I think that thing is so bad. We have not figured out yet how we're going to function in a society and get those occasional times when your phone is broken for three days or you go for a walk with no service. But I very much feel like my phone controls me, not the other way around. And I hate it, but I haven't figured out what to do about it.
turns.zh: 是啊，我认为那件事太糟糕了。我们还没有弄清楚如何在社会中运作，并得到那些偶尔的手机坏掉三天或散步没有信号的时刻。但我非常觉得是我的手机在控制我，而不是相反。我讨厌这样，但我还没想出办法。

turns.en: Well, that's what I'm worried about with future technology. This was so unanticipated. If you imagine a world when you can... imagine going up to someone in 1984 and pointing to a phone and saying, 'One day that'll be in your pocket, it's going to ruin your life.' Like, what? 'Yeah, one day people are going to be jerking off to that thing.' You're like, what? 'One day people are going to be watching people get murdered on Instagram.' I've seen so many murders on Instagram over the last few months. Really? I've never seen one. Been a bad timeline. Me and my friend Tom Segura, every morning we text each other the worst things that we find on Instagram. Why? For fun. He's a comedian, I'm both comedians. That's fun to you? Yeah, this is just ridiculous. I mean, just crazy car accidents, people get gored by bulls, and every... we try to top each other. So every day he's sending me the most... every day when I wake up, I'm like, 'Tom, what you got?'
turns.zh: 嗯，这就是我对未来技术的担忧。这是如此出乎意料。如果你想象一个世界，当你……想象一下在1984年走到一个人面前，指着一部手机说，‘有一天它会放在你的口袋里，它会毁了你的生活。’就像，什么？‘是啊，有一天人们会对着那东西手淫。’你会说，什么？‘有一天人们会在Instagram上看人被谋杀。’过去几个月我在Instagram上看到过很多谋杀案。真的吗？我从来没见过。你的时间线不好。我和我的朋友Tom Segura，每天早上我们互相发在Instagram上找到的最糟糕的东西。为什么？为了好玩。他是喜剧演员，我们都是喜剧演员。你觉得这好玩？是啊，这简直荒谬。我的意思是，疯狂的车祸，人被公牛顶伤，每一件……我们试图超越对方。所以每天他发给我最……每天我醒来，我就说，‘Tom，你找到什么了？’

transcript.sec: The appeal of dark content on Instagram

turns.en: Can you explain what's fun about that?
turns.zh: 你能解释一下那有什么好玩的吗？

turns.en: Well, he's a comic and I'm a comic, and comics like chaos. We like ridiculous, outrageous things that are just so far beyond the norm of what you experience in a regular day. Also, there's the understanding of the wide spectrum of human behavior. If you're a nice person and you surround yourself with nice people, you very rarely see someone get shot or stabbed for no reason randomly in the street. But on Instagram, you see that every day. There's something about that which reminds you, 'Oh, we're crazy.' The human species has a certain percentage of us that are just off the rails, out there causing chaos, jumping dirt bikes and landing on your neck. All that stuff is out there.
turns.zh: 嗯，他是个喜剧演员，我也是个喜剧演员，喜剧演员喜欢混乱。我们喜欢荒谬、离谱的事情，那些远远超出日常经历的事情。另外，还有对人类行为广泛谱系的理解。如果你是个好人，身边都是好人，你很少看到有人无缘无故在街上被枪击或刺伤。但在Instagram上，你每天都能看到。这其中有某种东西提醒你：‘哦，我们疯了。’人类物种中有一定比例的人就是失控了，在外面制造混乱，骑越野摩托车然后摔断脖子。所有这些事情都存在。

turns.en: Even hearing that makes me physically uncomfortable and unhappy to watch.
turns.zh: 即使听到这些，我身体上也会感到不舒服，看着也不开心。

turns.en: Oh yeah, it makes me uncomfortable too, but yet we do it to each other every day. It's not good, definitely not good, but I'm not going to stop. It's fun.
turns.zh: 哦，是的，也让我不舒服，但我们每天还是互相这样做。这不好，绝对不好，但我不会停止。这很有趣。

turns.en: But why is it fun?
turns.zh: 但为什么有趣呢？

turns.en: It's fun because it's my friend Tom, and we're both kind of the same in that way. We just look at this and that, and it's a thing we started doing a few months ago and can't stop. Instagram has learned that you do that, so it keeps showing you more and more. Instagram knows. When I go to the Discover page, it's just crazy. But the thing is, it shows up in your feed too. That's what I understand about the algorithm: it knows you're up, so it shows up in your feed even if they're not people I follow. Instagram shows them to me anyway.
turns.zh: 有趣是因为我的朋友汤姆，我们在这方面有点像。我们就是看看这个看看那个，这是几个月前开始做的事情，停不下来。Instagram已经学会了你这么做，所以不断给你展示更多。Instagram知道。我去发现页面时，简直疯了。但问题是，它也会出现在你的信息流里。这就是我对算法的理解：它知道你醒着，所以即使不是关注的人，也会出现在你的信息流里。Instagram还是会展示给我。

turns.en: I heard an interesting thing a few days ago about Instagram and the feed: if you use it at off hours when they have more processing capability available because fewer people are using it, you get better recommendations. So your feed will be better in the middle of the night. But what is 'better'? Doesn't your feed become more addictive to you? For me, better would be more murders, more animal attacks. Sounds horrible, but it seems to know that's what I like, what I interact with, so it sends me that most of the time.
turns.zh: 几天前我听到一个关于Instagram和信息流的有趣说法：如果你在非高峰时段使用，那时他们有更多处理能力，因为使用的人少，你会得到更好的推荐。所以你的信息流在半夜会更好。但什么是‘更好’？你的信息流不是变得更让你上瘾吗？对我来说，更好就是更多谋杀、更多动物袭击。听起来很可怕，但它似乎知道那是我喜欢的，是我互动的，所以大部分时间都推送给我。

turns.en: Yeah, that probably has all kinds of crazy psychological effects, I'm sure. That's also one of the reasons why I want to get rid of it and move away from it.
turns.zh: 是的，那可能产生各种疯狂的心理影响，我敢肯定。这也是我想摆脱它、远离它的原因之一。

turns.en: Maybe it went too far. I don't even know if it's too far, but what it is, is showing me the darker regions of society, of civilization, of human behavior.
turns.zh: 也许它走得太远了。我甚至不知道是不是太远了，但它确实在向我展示社会、文明、人类行为中更黑暗的区域。

turns.en: But you think we're about to edit all that out? I wonder if that is a solution. I really do, because I don't think it's outside the realm of possibility. If we really can engineer, like one of the talks about Neuralink that's really promising is people with spinal cord injuries, people that can't move their body, and being able to hotwire that where it essentially controls all these parts of your body that you couldn't control anymore. That would be an amazing thing for people that are injured, for people that are paralyzed, who have all sorts of neurological conditions. That is probably one of the first implementations, and that's what Elon has talked about as well. One of the first implementations is the restoration of sight, cognitive function enhanced for people that have brain issues. That's tremendously exciting.
turns.zh: 但你认为我们即将把所有这些都编辑掉吗？我想知道这是否是一个解决方案。我真的这么想，因为我不认为这超出了可能性范围。如果我们真的能设计，比如关于Neuralink的一个很有前景的讨论是脊髓损伤患者、无法移动身体的人，能够通过直接连接来控制那些你无法再控制的身体部位。这对受伤的人、瘫痪的人、有各种神经系统疾病的人来说将是了不起的事情。这可能是首批应用之一，埃隆也谈到过。首批应用之一是恢复视力，增强有脑部问题的人的认知功能。这非常令人兴奋。

turns.en: Yeah, and like many other technologies, I don't think we can stop neural interfaces because of the great good that's going to happen along the way. But I also don't think we know where it goes. It's Pandora's Box for sure, and I think when we open it, we're not going to go back, just like we're not going to go back to no computers without some natural disaster.
turns.zh: 是的，像许多其他技术一样，我认为我们无法阻止神经接口，因为沿途会有巨大的好处。但我也认为我们不知道它会走向何方。这绝对是潘多拉魔盒，我认为一旦打开，我们就回不去了，就像没有自然灾害我们不会回到没有电脑的时代一样。

turns.en: By the way, I mean this as a great compliment: you are one of the most neutral people I have ever heard talk about the merge coming. You're just like, 'Yeah, I think it's going to happen. It'll be good in these ways, bad in these ways.' But you seem unbelievably neutral about it, which is always something I admire.
turns.zh: 顺便说一句，我这么说是一种极大的赞美：你是我听过的谈论即将到来的融合时最中立的人之一。你就像，‘是的，我认为它会发生。它会在这些方面好，那些方面坏。’但你对此似乎难以置信地中立，这总是让我钦佩。

turns.en: I try to be as neutral about everything as possible, except for corruption, which I think is one of the most massive problems with the way our culture is governed. I think corruption and the influence of money are just giant terrible issues. But in terms of social issues and the way human beings believe and think about things, I try to be as neutral as possible because I think the only way to truly understand the way other people think is to try to look at it through their mind. If you have this inherent bias and a very rigid view of what's good and bad and right and wrong, I don't think that serves you very well for understanding why people differ. So I try to be as neutral and objective as possible when I look at anything. This is a skill I've learned; this is not something I had in 2009 when I started this podcast. I started this podcast just around with friends, and I had no idea what it was. There's no way I could have ever known, but also had no idea what it was going to do to me in terms of the evolution of me as a human being. I am so much nicer, so much more aware of things, so much more conscious of the way other people think and feel. I'm just a totally different person than I was in 2009, which is hard to recognize. It's hard to believe. That's really cool, but it's just an inevitable consequence of this unexpected education that I've received.
turns.zh: 我尽量对一切都保持中立，除了腐败，我认为这是我们文化治理方式中最严重的问题之一。我认为腐败和金钱的影响是巨大的可怕问题。但在社会问题和人类信仰与思考方式方面，我尽量保持中立，因为我认为真正理解他人想法的唯一方法是尝试通过他们的思维来看待。如果你有这种固有的偏见和非常僵化的好坏对错观，我认为这对理解人们为何不同没有帮助。所以我尽量在看待任何事情时保持中立和客观。这是我学会的一项技能；这不是我在2009年开始这个播客时就有的。我开始这个播客只是和朋友们一起，我完全不知道它是什么。我根本不可能知道，也不知道它对我作为人类的进化会产生什么影响。我变得好多了，对事物更加敏感，对他人想法和感受更加有意识。我和2009年的我完全是不同的人，这很难承认。难以置信。这真的很酷，但这只是我接受这种意外教育的必然结果。

turns.en: Did the empathy kind of come on linearly?
turns.zh: 同理心是线性增长的吗？

turns.en: No, it was not linear. It came on recognizing that the negative interactions on social media that I was doing didn't help me, didn't help the person. And then having compassion for this person that's up or done something stupid. It's not good to just dunk on people. There's no benefit there other than to give you some sort of social credit and get a bunch of likes. It didn't make me feel good. That's not good. And also, a lot of psychedelics, a ton of psychedelic experiences from 2009 on, gave me a greater understanding of the impact. I had one recently, and the overwhelming message that I was getting through this was that everything I say and do ripples off into all the people that I interact with. And if I'm not doing something with at least the goal of overall good or overall understanding, then I'm doing bad. And that bad is a real thing, as much as you try to ignore it because you don't interface with it instantly.
turns.zh: 不，不是线性的。它始于认识到我在社交媒体上的负面互动对我没有帮助，对那个人也没有帮助。然后对那个做了蠢事的人产生同情。仅仅贬低别人是不好的。除了获得某种社会认可和一堆点赞之外，没有任何好处。那并没有让我感觉良好。那不好。而且，从2009年开始，大量的迷幻体验让我更深刻地理解了影响。最近有一次，我通过它得到的压倒性信息是，我所说和所做的一切都会波及到我与之互动的所有人。如果我不以至少整体善或整体理解为目标做事，那我就是在做坏事。而那种坏是真实存在的，尽管你试图忽视它，因为你不会立即感受到它。

turns.en: You're still creating unnecessary negativity and that I should avoid that as much as possible. It was like an overwhelming message that this psychedelic experience was giving me. And I took it because I was just particularly anxious that day about the state of the world, particularly anxious about Ukraine and Russia and China and the political system that we have in this country, and this incredibly polarizing way that the left and the right engage with each other. God, it just seems so tormented. So I was just some days I just think too much about it. I'm like, I need something to crack me out of this. So I took these psychedelics.
turns.zh: 你还在制造不必要的负面情绪，我应该尽可能避免。这是那次迷幻体验给我的一个压倒性信息。我服用它是因为那天我对世界局势特别焦虑，尤其担心乌克兰、俄罗斯、中国，以及我们国家的政治体制，还有左派和右派之间那种极度两极分化的互动方式。天啊，这一切看起来太痛苦了。有些日子我就是想太多。我觉得我需要什么东西来打破这种状态，所以我服用了这些迷幻药。

turns.en: Are you surprised psychedelic therapy has not made, from what you thought would happen in the early 2010s till now, are you surprised it has not made more progress on a path to legalization as a medical achievement?
turns.zh: 从你在2010年代初的预期到现在，迷幻疗法没有取得更多进展，你对此感到惊讶吗？作为一项医学成就，它在合法化道路上没有取得更大进展，你惊讶吗？

turns.en: No, I'm not, because there's a lot of people that don't want it to be in place, and those people have tremendous power over our medical system and over our regulatory system. And those people have also not experienced these psychedelics. There's very few people that have experienced profound psychedelic experiences that don't think there's an overall good for those things. So the problem is you're having these laws and these rules implemented by people who are completely ignorant about the positive effects of these things. And if you know the history of psychedelic prohibition in this country, it all took place during 1970, and it was really to stop the civil rights movement and the anti-war movement. They tried to find a way to make all these things that these people were doing, that was causing them to think in these very different ways, like 'tune in, turn on, drop out.' They just wanted to put a halt to that. What better way to lock everyone who participates in that in a cage? Find the people producing it, lock them in cages, put them in jail for the rest of their life, make sure it's illegal, arrest people, put the busts on television, make sure that people are aware. And then you also connect it to drugs that are inherently dangerous for society and detrimental, the fentanyl crisis, the crack cocaine crisis that we experienced in the 90s. All of those things are under the blanket of drugs. Psychedelic drugs are also talked about like drugs, even though they have these profound spiritual and psychological changes that they emit. I remember when I was in elementary school in drug education, they talked about marijuana being really bad because it's a gateway to these other things, and there's this bad one, that bad one, heroin, whatever. And the very end of the line, the worst possible thing, is LSD. Did you take LSD and go, 'Oh, they're lying.' Psychedelic therapy was definitely one of the most important things in my life. And I assumed, given how powerful it was for me, like I struggled with all kinds of anxiety and other negative things, and to watch all of that go away, like I traveled to another country for a week, did a few things, came back a totally different person. And I was like, 'I've been lied to my whole life.' Yeah, I'm so grateful that this happened to me now. Talked to a bunch of other people, all similar experiences. I assumed this was a while ago, I assumed it was, and I was very interested in what was happening in the US. I was particularly looking at where MDMA and psilocybin were on the path, and I was like, 'All right, this is going to get through, and this is going to change the mental health of a lot of people in a really positive way.' And I am surprised we have not made faster progress there, but I'm still optimistic we will.
turns.zh: 不，我不惊讶，因为有很多人不希望它实现，而这些人对我们的医疗系统和监管系统拥有巨大的权力。而且这些人也没有体验过这些迷幻药。很少有经历过深刻迷幻体验的人不认为这些东西总体上是有益的。所以问题在于，这些法律和规则是由完全不了解这些东西积极影响的人来执行的。如果你了解这个国家迷幻药禁令的历史，这一切都发生在1970年，实际上是为了阻止民权运动和反战运动。他们试图找到一种方法来阻止这些人所做的那些让他们以非常不同的方式思考的事情，比如‘调谐、开启、退出’。他们只是想阻止这一切。还有什么比把每个参与其中的人都关进笼子更好的办法呢？找到生产它的人，把他们关进笼子，终身监禁，确保它是非法的，逮捕人，在电视上播放突袭行动，确保人们都知道。然后你还把它与对社会固有危险和有害的药物联系起来，比如芬太尼危机、90年代的可卡因危机。所有这些都被归在‘毒品’这个总称下。迷幻药也被当作毒品来谈论，尽管它们能带来深刻的精神和心理变化。我记得上小学时接受毒品教育，他们说大麻很糟糕，因为它是通往其他东西的‘入门毒品’，还有这个坏的那个坏的，海洛因什么的。而最糟糕的、最坏的东西就是LSD。你吃了LSD就会想：‘哦，他们在撒谎。’迷幻疗法绝对是我生命中最重要的事情之一。鉴于它对我有多强大——我曾与各种焦虑和其他负面情绪作斗争，然后看着这一切消失——我去了另一个国家一周，做了几件事，回来时完全变了一个人。我当时想：‘我这辈子都被骗了。’是的，我很感激这件事发生在我身上。我和其他很多人聊过，都有类似的经历。我假设这是很久以前的事了，我确实这么认为，而且我对美国正在发生的事情非常感兴趣。我特别关注MDMA和裸盖菇素的进展，我想：‘好吧，这会通过的，这会以非常积极的方式改变很多人的心理健康。’我确实惊讶于我们在这方面没有取得更快的进展，但我仍然乐观地认为我们会取得进展。

turns.en: Well, we have made so much progress from the time of the 1990s. In the 1990s, you never heard about psychedelic retreats, you never heard about people taking these vacations, you never heard about people getting together in groups and doing these things and coming back with these profound experiences that they relay to other people, and literally seeing people change, seeing who they are change, seeing people become less selfish, less toxic, less mean, and more empathetic and more understanding. Yeah, it's I mean, I can only talk about it from a personal experience. It's been a radical change in my life, as well as again having all these conversations with different people. I feel so fortunate to be able to do this, that I've had so many different conversations with so many different people that think so differently, and so many exceptional people that have accomplished so many incredible things. And you get to sort of understand the way their mind works, the way they see the world, the way they interface with things. It's awesome, it's pretty cool. And that is one of the cooler things about being a human, that you can find a way to mitigate all the negative aspects of the monkey body, and that there are tools that are in place. But unfortunately, in this very prohibitive society, this society of prohibition, we're denied those, and we're denied ones that have never killed anybody, which is really bizarre when you know OxyContin can still be prescribed. What's the deal with why we can't make, like why we can't get these medicines that have transformed people's lives more available? What's the deal with why we can't stop the opioid crisis or fentanyl? Seems like just an unbelievable crisis for San Francisco. You remember at the beginning of the conversation when you said that AI will do a lot of good, overall good, but also no harm. If we legalize drugs, all drugs, that would do the same thing. Would you advocate to legalize all drugs?
turns.zh: 嗯，从1990年代开始我们已经取得了很大进步。在1990年代，你从未听说过迷幻静修，从未听说过人们去度这种假，从未听说过人们聚在一起做这些事情，然后带着深刻的体验回来告诉别人，并且亲眼看到人们改变，看到他们的本质改变，看到人们变得不那么自私、不那么有毒、不那么刻薄，而是更有同理心、更善解人意。是的，我只能从个人经历来说。这给我的生活带来了彻底的改变，也让我与不同的人进行了所有这些对话。我感到非常幸运能够做到这一点，我与这么多不同的人进行了这么多不同的对话，他们的思维方式如此不同，还有这么多杰出的人取得了如此多不可思议的成就。你开始理解他们的大脑如何运作，他们如何看待世界，他们如何与事物互动。这太棒了，非常酷。这是作为人类最酷的事情之一，你可以找到一种方法来减轻猴子身体的所有负面影响，而且有现成的工具。但不幸的是，在这个非常禁止的社会，这个禁止的社会里，我们被剥夺了这些，而且我们被剥夺了那些从未杀死过任何人的东西，这真的很奇怪，因为奥施康定仍然可以开处方。为什么我们不能让这些改变了人们生活的药物更容易获得？为什么我们不能阻止阿片类药物危机或芬太尼？对旧金山来说，这似乎是一场难以置信的危机。你还记得在对话开始时，你说人工智能会带来很多好处，总体上是好的，但也不会造成伤害。如果我们合法化所有毒品，那也会产生同样的效果。你会主张合法化所有毒品吗？

turns.en: It's a very complicated question, because I think you're going to have a lot of addicts that wouldn't be addicts. You're going to have a lot of people's lives destroyed because it's legal. There's a lot of people that may not be psychologically capable of handling things. Maybe they already have, like that's the thing about psychedelics: they do not ever recommend them for people that have a slippery grasp on reality as it is. For sure, people that are struggling, people that are already on a bunch of medications that allow them to just keep a steady state of existence in the normal world, if you just bombard them with psilocybin, who knows what kind of an effect that's going to have, and whether or not they're psychologically too fragile to recover from that. I mean, there's many, many stories of people taking too much acid and never coming back. Yeah, yeah. These are like a powerful doesn't seem to like begin to cover it, right? Yeah. But there's also what is it about humans that are constantly looking to perturb their normal state of consciousness, constantly? Whether it's we're both drinking coffee, you know, people smoke cigarettes, they take Adderall, they do all...
turns.zh: 这是一个非常复杂的问题，因为我认为会有很多成瘾者本来不会成为成瘾者。会有很多人的生活因为合法化而被毁掉。有很多人可能在心理上无法处理这些事情。也许他们已经这样了，这就是迷幻药的问题：它们从不推荐给那些对现实把握不稳的人。当然，那些正在挣扎的人，那些已经服用大量药物以维持正常世界稳定状态的人，如果你直接用裸盖菇素轰炸他们，谁知道会产生什么效果，以及他们是否在心理上过于脆弱而无法恢复。我的意思是，有很多很多关于人们服用过量LSD而再也回不来的故事。是的，是的。这些药力强大得似乎都不足以形容，对吧？是的。但还有一点，人类为什么总是想要扰乱他们正常的意识状态？总是这样。无论是我们都在喝咖啡，你知道，人们抽烟，他们服用阿德拉，他们做各种……

turns.en: People do all sorts of things to change and enhance their normal state of consciousness. Whether it's meditation or yoga, they're always doing something to get out of their own way or distract themselves from the pain of existence. It seems like a normal part of humans, and even monkeys—vervet monkeys get addicted to alcohol, they get addicted to fermented fruits and alcohol, and they become drunks and alcoholics. What do you think is the deep lesson there?
turns.zh: 人们做各种各样的事情来改变和增强他们的正常意识状态。无论是冥想还是瑜伽，他们总是在做些什么来摆脱自己的束缚或分散自己对存在痛苦的注意力。这似乎是人类正常的一部分，甚至猴子——黑长尾猴会对酒精上瘾，它们对发酵的水果和酒精上瘾，然后变成醉鬼和酒鬼。你认为这背后的深层教训是什么？

turns.en: Well, we're not happy exactly, you know. And some things can make you happy for a little bit—like a couple of drinks make you happy for a little while, until you become an alcoholic, until you destroy your liver, until you crash your car, until you're involved in some violent encounter you'd never be in if you weren't drunk. I love caffeine, which is clearly a drug. Alcohol I like, but I often think, 'Yeah, this is like, ding me, I wish I hadn't had this drink.' And other things I mostly choose to avoid. But that's because you're smart, and you're probably aware of the pros and cons, and how it affects you, what's good and what's detrimental. But that's a decision you can make as an informed human being, which you're not allowed to make if everything's illegal.
turns.zh: 嗯，我们并不完全快乐，你知道。有些东西能让你快乐一小会儿——比如喝几杯能让你开心一阵子，直到你变成酒鬼，直到你毁了肝脏，直到你撞车，直到你卷入一些如果你没喝醉绝不会参与的暴力冲突。我喜欢咖啡因，它显然是一种毒品。我喜欢喝酒，但我常常想，‘是啊，这就像，哎呀，我真希望没喝这杯。’其他东西我大多选择避免。但那是因为你聪明，你可能知道利弊，知道它如何影响你，什么对你有益，什么有害。但那是你作为一个知情的人可以做出的决定，如果一切都是非法的，你就不被允许做这个决定。

turns.en: Right, and also when things are illegal, criminals sell those things because you're not tempering the desire by making it illegal; you're just making access much more complicated. What I was going to say is that fentanyl is really great—I don't want to know, apparently it is. Peter Berg was on the podcast, he produced that painkiller documentary for Netflix about the Sackler family. He said he took OxyContin once recreationally and was like, 'Oh my God, it's amazing. Keep this away from me, it feels so good.' That's part of the problem: it will wreck your life, it will capture you, but the feeling is unbelievable. How did Lenny Bruce describe it? I think he described heroin as getting a warm hug from God. The feeling it gives you is probably pretty spectacular. I don't know if legalizing that will solve the problems, but I do know that another problem we're not paying attention to is the rise of the cartels. Right across our border, there are enormous organizations that make untold amounts of money. They put fentanyl in street Xanax, people overdose thinking they're getting Xanax and die from fentanyl. They do it with cocaine, with everything. Fentanyl is cheap and insanely potent, and that wouldn't be a problem if things were legal. So would you net out towards saying, 'Let's just legalize everything'?
turns.zh: 对，而且当东西非法时，罪犯会卖这些东西，因为通过使其非法并不能抑制欲望；你只是让获取变得更加复杂。我想说的是，芬太尼真的很棒——我不想亲身体验，但显然它很棒。彼得·伯格上过播客，他为Netflix制作了关于萨克勒家族的止痛药纪录片。他说他曾经娱乐性地服用过一次奥施康定，然后说，‘哦，天哪，太神奇了。离我远点，感觉太好了。’这是问题的一部分：它会毁掉你的生活，它会抓住你，但那种感觉难以置信。莱尼·布鲁斯是怎么描述的？我想他把海洛因描述成得到上帝温暖的拥抱。它给你的感觉可能相当壮观。我不知道合法化是否能解决问题，但我确实知道另一个我们没有注意的问题是卡特尔组织的崛起。就在我们的边境对面，有庞大的组织赚取数不清的钱。他们把芬太尼掺进街头的Xanax里，人们以为自己在吃Xanax却过量服用芬太尼而死。他们对可卡因也这样做，对一切东西都这样。芬太尼便宜且极其强效，如果东西是合法的，这就不是问题。那么，你最终会倾向于说，‘让我们干脆让一切合法化’吗？

turns.en: I would net out towards that, but I would also put into place some serious mitigation efforts, like counseling for drug addiction and ibogaine therapy, which is another thing that's illegal. Someone was just telling me about how transformative it was for them. I haven't experienced it personally, but ibogaine for many of my friends who have had pill problems—my friend Ed Clay started an ibogaine center in Mexico because he had an injury, got hooked on pills, and couldn't kick it. He did ibogaine once, one time, done. A 25-hour super uncomfortable experience—it's supposed to be horrible. It's not recreational, not something you want to do on the weekend with friends. You do it because you need to figure out how to get out of this mess. Think about how much money is spent on rehabs in this country, and what's the relapse rate? It's really high. I have many friends who have been to rehab for drug and alcohol abuse, and quite a few went right back to it. It doesn't seem to be that effective. It seems effective when people hit rock bottom, have a strong will, get involved in a 12-step program or Narcotics Anonymous, get support from others, and eventually build a foundation of other behaviors—exercise, meditation, yoga, whatever becomes their new addiction, a more positive one. But the reality of physical addiction is that there are mitigation efforts. So many people have taken psilocybin and completely quit drugs, quit cigarettes, because they realize, 'Oh, this is why I'm doing this.' That's why I was more optimistic that the world would have made faster progress towards acceptance. You hear so many stories like this. So I would say, clearly a lot of our existing mental health treatment at best doesn't work, clearly our addiction programs are ineffective. If we have this thing that in every scientific study delivers these unbelievable results, it's going to happen. I still am excited for it, I still think it'll be a transformatively positive development. It will change politics, it will change the way we think of other human beings, it will change the way we think of society and culture as a whole, it will change the way people interact with each other if it becomes legalized. It's slowly becoming legalized—think of marijuana, the gateway drug. Marijuana is now legal recreationally in 23 states, and medically in many more. It's really easy to get a medical license in California. I got one in 1996. You used to be able to just go somewhere and say, 'I have a headache,' and bam, you get a legal prescription for weed. I used to have to go to Englewood, to the hood, to the Englewood Wellness Center. I thought, 'This is crazy.' Marijuana is now kind of sort of legal. Then in 2016 it became legal recreationally in California, and it changed everything. I have all these people who were right-wing, taking edibles to sleep.
turns.zh: 我最终会倾向于那个方向，但我也会实施一些严肃的缓解措施，比如药物成瘾咨询和伊博格碱疗法，这是另一种非法的东西。有人刚刚告诉我这对他们来说是多么具有变革性。我没有亲身经历过，但伊博格碱对我许多有药丸问题的朋友来说——我的朋友埃德·克莱在墨西哥开了一个伊博格碱中心，因为他受伤了，对药丸上瘾，无法戒掉。他做了一次伊博格碱，一次，就戒掉了。一次25小时的超级不舒服的经历——据说很可怕。这不是娱乐性的，不是你想在周末和朋友一起做的事情。你做是因为你需要想办法摆脱这个烂摊子。想想这个国家在康复中心上花了多少钱，复发率是多少？非常高。我有很多朋友去过戒毒和戒酒康复中心，不少人都直接回去了。似乎效果不太好。当人们跌入谷底，有坚强的意志，参加12步计划或匿名戒毒会，从他人那里获得支持，最终建立其他行为的基础——锻炼、冥想、瑜伽，无论什么成为他们新的成瘾，一个更积极的成瘾——这时似乎有效。但身体成瘾的现实是，有缓解措施。很多人服用了裸盖菇素后完全戒掉了毒品，戒掉了香烟，因为他们意识到，‘哦，这就是我这样做的原因。’这就是为什么我更乐观地认为世界会在接受方面取得更快的进展。你听到很多这样的故事。所以我会说，显然我们现有的许多心理健康治疗充其量是无效的，显然我们的成瘾项目是无效的。如果我们有这个东西，在每一项科学研究中都显示出这些令人难以置信的结果，它就会发生。我仍然对此感到兴奋，我仍然认为这将是一个变革性的积极发展。它将改变政治，它将改变我们看待他人的方式，它将改变我们对整个社会和文化的看法，如果它合法化，它将改变人们相互互动的方式。它正在慢慢合法化——想想大麻，这个入门毒品。大麻现在在23个州是娱乐性合法的，在更多州是医疗合法的。在加州获得医疗执照非常容易。我在1996年就拿到了一个。过去你可以去某个地方说，‘我头疼’，然后砰，你就得到了合法的医用大麻处方。我以前得去恩格尔伍德，去贫民区，去恩格尔伍德健康中心。我想，‘这太疯狂了。’大麻现在算是有点合法了。然后在2016年，它在加州娱乐性合法化，这改变了一切。我有所有这些右翼人士，他们吃大麻食品来助眠。

turns.en: Like this is because now that it's legal they thought about it in a different way. And I think that that drug, which is a fairly mild psychedelic, also has enhancing effects. It makes people more compassionate, it makes people more kind and friendly. It's sort of the opposite of a drug that enhances violence. It doesn't enhance violence at all. It just does—like alcohol does, that cocaine does. To say a thing that'll make me very unpopular: I hate marijuana. It does not sit well with me at all.
turns.zh: 就像这样，因为现在它合法了，他们以不同的方式看待它。我认为那种药物，一种相当温和的迷幻药，也有增强效果。它让人们更有同情心，更善良友好。它几乎是暴力增强药物的反面。它根本不增强暴力。它就像酒精、可卡因那样。说一件会让我非常不受欢迎的事：我讨厌大麻。它对我完全不适合。

turns.en: What does it do to you that you don't like?
turns.zh: 它对你做了什么让你不喜欢？

turns.en: Uh, it makes me tired and slow for a long time after it.
turns.zh: 呃，它让我在之后很长一段时间里感到疲倦和迟钝。

turns.en: I think also there's biological variability, right? Like some people, my wife, she does not do well with alcohol. She can get drunk off one drink. But it's biological. She has some sort of a genetic—I forget what it's called, something about Billy Rubin? Something that her body just doesn't process alcohol very well. So she's a cheap date.
turns.zh: 我认为也存在生物差异，对吧？比如有些人，我妻子，她对酒精反应不好。她喝一杯就醉。但这是生物性的。她有某种基因——我忘了叫什么，关于胆红素？她的身体就是不太能处理酒精。所以她是个便宜约会对象。

turns.en: Oh, all I meant is that genetically I got whatever the mutation is that makes it an unpleasant experience. Yeah, what I was saying is for me that's the opposite. Alcohol doesn't bother me at all. I could drink three or four drinks and I'm sober in 20 minutes. My body, my liver just blasts right through it. I can sober up real quick. But I also don't need it. Like I'm doing Sober October, for the whole month. I did shows last night, great, no problems. Not having alcohol doesn't seem to bother me at all. But I do like it. I do like a glass of wine. It's a nice thing at the end of the day. I like it.
turns.zh: 哦，我的意思是基因上我有某种突变，让这成为一种不愉快的体验。是的，我说的是对我来说正好相反。酒精对我完全没有影响。我可以喝三四杯，20分钟内就清醒了。我的身体，我的肝脏就像直接把它处理掉了。我能很快清醒。但我也不需要它。比如我在做“清醒十月”，整个月都不喝酒。昨晚做了演出，很棒，没问题。不喝酒似乎完全不影响我。但我确实喜欢它。我确实喜欢一杯葡萄酒。这是一天结束时美好的事情。我喜欢它。

turns.en: Speaking of that and psychedelics in general, you know, many cultures have had a place for some sort of psychedelic time in someone's life or rite of passage. But as far as I can tell, most of them are under—there's some sort of ritual about it. And I do worry that—and I think these things are so powerful—that I worry about them just being kind of used all over the place all the time.
turns.zh: 说到这个和一般的迷幻药，你知道，许多文化在人的一生或成人礼中都有某种迷幻时刻的位置。但据我所知，大多数都有某种仪式。我确实担心——而且我认为这些东西太强大了——我担心它们被到处随意使用。

turns.en: Yeah, and I hope that we as a society—because I think this is not going to happen even if it's slow—find a way to treat this with the respect that it needs. Um, yeah, see how that goes. Agreed.
turns.zh: 是的，我希望我们作为一个社会——因为我认为即使进展缓慢，这也不会发生——找到一种方式来给予它应有的尊重。嗯，是的，看看情况如何。同意。

turns.en: Yeah, I think set and setting is very important, and thinking about what you're doing before you're doing it, while you're doing it. Like I was saying the other night when I had this psychedelic experience, I was just like, God, sometimes I just think too much about the world and that it's so—and you have kids and you wonder like what kind of a world are they going to grow up in? And it was just one of those days where I was just like, God, there's so much anger and there's so much this and that. And then it just took it away from me the rest of the day. Like that night I was so friendly and so happy and I just wanted to hug everybody. I just really—I got it. I got—oh my God, that's not thinking about it wrong.
turns.zh: 是的，我认为环境和心态非常重要，在做之前、做的时候都要思考你在做什么。就像我前几天晚上那次迷幻体验，我当时想，天啊，有时候我就是想太多关于世界的事，它如此——而且你有孩子，你会想他们将在什么样的世界里长大？那天就是那种日子，我想，天啊，有这么多愤怒，这么多这个那个。然后它就把这些从我身上带走了，一整天剩下的时间。那天晚上我非常友好、非常快乐，就想拥抱每个人。我真的——我明白了。我——哦天哪，那并不是想错了。

turns.en: Do you think the anger in the world is way higher than it used to be, or we just—it's like all these dynamics from social media we were talking about?
turns.zh: 你认为世界上的愤怒比以前高得多，还是我们只是——就像我们讨论的社交媒体带来的这些动态？

turns.en: I think the dynamics in social media have certainly exacerbated anger in some people. But I think anger in the world is just a part of frustration in inequality, problems that are so clear but are not solved, and all the issues that people have. I mean, it's not a coincidence that a lot of the mass violence that you're seeing in this country, mass looting and all these different things, are being done by poor people.
turns.zh: 我认为社交媒体的动态确实加剧了一些人的愤怒。但我认为世界上的愤怒只是不平等带来的挫败感的一部分，那些问题如此明显却得不到解决，以及人们所有的困扰。我的意思是，你在这个国家看到的许多大规模暴力、大规模抢劫等等，都是由穷人实施的，这并非巧合。

turns.en: Do you think AGI will be an equalizing force for the world or further inequality?
turns.zh: 你认为AGI会成为世界的均衡力量还是加剧不平等？

turns.en: I think it would depend on how it's implemented. My concern is again what we were talking about before with some sort of neural interface, that it will increase your ability to be productive to a point where you can control resources so much more than anyone else, and you will be able to advance your economic portfolio and your influence on the world through that, your amount of power that you can acquire. It will before the other people can get involved. Because I would imagine financially it'll be like cell phones in the beginning. You remember when the movie Wall Street when he had that big brick cell phone? It's like, look at him, he's out there on the beach with a phone. That was crazy. No one had one of those things back then, and they were so rare. I got one in 1994 when I first moved to California, and I thought I was living in the future. No, it was a Motorola StarTAC. That was a cool phone. I actually had one in my car in 1988. I was one of the first people to get a cell phone. I got one in my car, and it was great because my friend Bill Blumenreich, who runs the Comedy Connection, he would call me because he knew he could get a hold of me. Like if someone got sick or fell out, I could get a gig because he could call me. So I was in my car, he's like, "Joe, what are you doing? Do you have a spot tonight?" And I'm like, "No, I'm open." He's like, "Fantastic." And so he'd give me gigs. So I got a bunch of gigs through this phone where it kind of paid itself. But I got it just because it was cool. Like I could drive down the street and call people. Dude, I'm driving and I'm calling you. It was nuts to be able to drive and I had a little antenna, a little squiggly antenna on my car on the roof. But now everyone has one. You can go to a third world country and people in small villages have phones. It's super common, it's everywhere essentially. More people have phones than don't have phones. There are more phones than there are human beings, which is pretty wild. And I think that initial cost problem—it's going to be prohibitively expensive initially. And the problem is the wealthy people are going to be able to do that, and then the real crazy ones that wind up getting the holes drilled in their head. And if that stuff is effective—maybe it's not generation one, maybe there are problems with generation one, but generation two is better—there's going to be a time where you have to enter into the game. There's going to be a time where you have to sell your stocks, like you know, don't wait too long. Hang on, there go. And once that happens, my concern is that the people that have that will have such a massive advantage over everyone else that the gap between the haves and have-nots will be even further and it'll be more polarized.
turns.zh: 我认为这取决于如何实施。我的担忧又回到了我们之前讨论的某种神经接口，它会将你的生产力提升到远超他人的程度，让你能控制更多资源，并通过它提升你的经济组合和世界影响力，以及你能获得的权力。它会在其他人参与之前就做到。因为我想象在财务上它会像早期的手机。你还记得电影《华尔街》里他拿着那个大砖头手机吗？就像，看他，他在海滩上拿着电话。那太疯狂了。那时没人有那种东西，它们非常稀有。1994年我第一次搬到加州时买了一个，我觉得自己活在未来。不，那是摩托罗拉StarTAC。那是个很酷的手机。实际上1988年我就在车里装了一个。我是最早拥有手机的人之一。我在车里装了一个，这很棒，因为我的朋友比尔·布鲁门瑞奇，他经营喜剧连线，他会打电话给我，因为他知道能联系到我。比如如果有人生病或退出，我就能接到演出，因为他能打电话给我。所以我在车里，他说：“乔，你在干嘛？今晚有空位吗？”我说：“没有，我有空。”他说：“太棒了。”然后他就给我演出。所以我通过这个电话得到了很多演出，它基本上自己付了钱。但我买它只是因为酷。比如我可以开车时打电话。老兄，我在开车，我在给你打电话。能开车打电话太疯狂了，我车上有个小天线，车顶上一个弯弯曲曲的小天线。但现在每个人都有。你可以去第三世界国家，小村庄里的人都有手机。这非常普遍，基本上无处不在。有手机的人比没有手机的人多。手机数量比人类数量还多，这相当疯狂。我认为最初的成本问题——一开始它会贵得令人望而却步。问题是富人能够做到，然后真正疯狂的人最终会在头上钻孔。如果那东西有效——也许不是第一代，第一代可能有问题，但第二代更好——总会有一个你必须进入游戏的时候。总会有一个你必须卖掉股票的时候，就像你知道的，别等太久。等等，就这样。一旦发生，我担心拥有它的人会比其他人有巨大优势，贫富差距会更大，更加两极分化。

turns.en: This is something I've changed my mind on. You know, someone at OpenAI said to me a few years ago, like, you really can't just let some people emerge without a plan, because it could be such an incredible distortion of power, and that we're going to have to have some sort of societal discussion about this.
turns.zh: 这是我改变想法的一件事。你知道，几年前OpenAI的一个人对我说，你真的不能在没有计划的情况下让一些人崛起，因为这可能会造成难以置信的权力扭曲，我们将不得不就此进行某种社会讨论。

turns.en: Yeah, that seems real. That seems like—so at this point, especially if it's as effective as AGI is, or as, excuse me, ChatGPT is. ChatGPT is so amazing. When you enter into it information, you ask it questions and it can give you answers, and you could ask it to code a website for you and it doesn't instantly—
turns.zh: 是的，这看起来是真的。看起来——所以在这一点上，尤其是如果它像AGI一样有效，或者像，抱歉，ChatGPT一样。ChatGPT太棒了。当你输入信息，问它问题，它能给你答案，你可以让它为你编写一个网站，它不会立即——

transcript.sec: Concerns about unequal access to advanced AI

turns.en: It solves problems that would literally take you decades to solve, and it gets to it right away. This is the dumbest it will ever be. Yeah, that's what's crazy. So imagine something like that, but even more advanced, multiple stages of improvement and innovation forward, and then it interfaces directly with the mind. But it only does it with the people that can afford it. And those people are just regular humans, so they haven't been enhanced. We haven't evolved physically; we still have all the human reward systems in place. We're still basically these territorial primates. And now you have, you know, just imagine some psychotic billionaire who gets this implant and decides to completely hijack our financial systems, acquire all the resources, set into place regulations and influences that only benefit them, and then make sure that they can control it from their...
turns.zh: 它能解决那些你本来需要几十年才能解决的问题，而且立刻就能搞定。这是它最笨的时候了。没错，这就是疯狂之处。所以想象一下，比这更先进的东西，经过多阶段的改进和创新，然后它直接与大脑连接。但只有付得起钱的人才能用。而那些人只是普通人类，并没有被增强。我们还没有进化，身体上还是老样子，仍然保留着所有人类奖励系统。我们基本上还是那些领地意识强烈的灵长类动物。现在你想想，某个精神病亿万富翁植入了这个设备，然后决定彻底劫持我们的金融系统，获取所有资源，制定只对自己有利的法规和影响力，并确保他们能控制这一切……

turns.en: How much do you think this actually requires a physical implant or a physical merge, versus just some people have access to GPT-7 and can spend a lot on the inference compute for it and some don't? I think that's going to be very transformative too. But my thought is that once we have a neural enhancement, if you think about the human mind and how it interacts with the world, how you interact with language and thoughts and facts, and something exponentially more powerful than that, but it also allows you to use the same emotions, the same ego, the same desires and drives—jealousy, lust, hate, anger—all of those things, but with this godlike power. When one person can read minds and other people can't, when one person has a completely accurate forecast of all the trends in stocks, resources, and commodities, and they can make choices based on those... I totally see all of that. The only thing I feel a little confused about is that human talking and listening bandwidth, or typing and reading bandwidth, is not very high. But it's high enough where if you can just say, 'Tell me everything that's going to happen in the stock market; if I want to go make all the money, what should I do right now?' It goes and just shows you on the screen. Even without a neural interface, you're kind of a lot of the way to the scenario you're describing. Sure, with stocks or with, you know, 'Tell me how to invent some new technology that will change the course of history.' Yeah, all those things. I think what somehow matters is access to massive amounts of computing power, or especially differentially massive amounts, maybe more than the interface itself. I think that certainly is going to play a massive factor in the amount of power and influence a human being has, having access to that. My concern is that what neural interfaces are going to do is now you're not a human mind interacting with that data; now you are some massively advanced version of what a human mind is, and it has profound possibilities that we can't even imagine. We can imagine, but we can't truly conceptualize them because we don't have the context, we don't have that ability and that possibility currently. We can just guess. But when it does get implemented, you're dealing with a completely different being. The only true thing I can say is I don't know.
turns.zh: 你认为这在多大程度上真的需要物理植入或物理融合，而不仅仅是有些人能用上 GPT-7，花很多钱在推理算力上，有些人则不能？我觉得那也会带来巨大的变革。但我的想法是，一旦有了神经增强，想想人类心智以及它如何与世界互动，你如何与语言、思想和事实互动，而某种东西比这强大指数级，但它还允许你使用同样的情感、同样的自我、同样的欲望和驱动力——嫉妒、欲望、仇恨、愤怒——所有这些，但带着神一般的力量。当一个人能读心而其他人不能，当一个人能完全准确地预测股票、资源和商品的所有趋势，并据此做出选择……我完全理解这些。我唯一有点困惑的是，人类说话和听力的带宽，或者打字和阅读的带宽，并不高。但已经足够高了，如果你能直接说：‘告诉我股市会发生什么；如果我想赚大钱，现在该怎么做？’它就会在屏幕上显示给你。即使没有神经接口，你离你描述的场景也已经很近了。当然，对于股票，或者‘告诉我如何发明一项改变历史进程的新技术’，所有这些事情。我认为真正重要的是获取大量算力，尤其是差异巨大的算力，也许比接口本身更重要。我认为这肯定会在一个人拥有的权力和影响力中发挥巨大作用。我的担忧是，神经接口会带来的结果是，你不再是一个人类心智与数据交互；你现在是人类心智的一个超级先进版本，它有着我们甚至无法想象的深远可能性。我们可以想象，但无法真正概念化，因为我们没有背景，目前没有那种能力和可能性。我们只能猜测。但当它真的实现时，你面对的是一个完全不同的存在。我唯一能说的实话是：我不知道。

transcript.sec: Reflections on being at the forefront of change

turns.en: Do you wonder why it's you? Do you ever think, 'How am I at the forefront of this spectacular change?'
turns.zh: 你有没有想过为什么是你？你有没有想过，‘我怎么就处在这场惊人变革的前沿了？’

turns.en: Well, first of all, I think this is very much like—you could make this statement for many companies, but none is it as true as for OpenAI. The CEO is far from the most important person in the company. In our case, there's a large handful of researchers, each of which are individually more critical to the success we've had so far and that we will have in the future than me. But even that, and I bet those people really are like, 'Hmm, this is weird to be them.' But it's certainly weird enough for me that it ups my simulation hypothesis probability somewhat.
turns.zh: 嗯，首先，我认为这很像——你可以对很多公司说这句话，但没有哪家公司像 OpenAI 这样真实。CEO 远不是公司里最重要的人。在我们这里，有一大批研究人员，每个人单独来看，对我们迄今为止以及未来的成功都比我更关键。但即便如此，我敢打赌那些人也会想，‘嗯，成为他们真奇怪。’但对我来说，这已经足够奇怪了，以至于它稍微提高了我对模拟假设的概率估计。

turns.en: When you think about the possibility of simulation theory, what percentage would you give?
turns.zh: 当你想到模拟理论的可能性时，你会给多少百分比？

turns.en: I've never known how to put any number on it. Every argument I've read explaining why it's super high probability all seems reasonable to me. It feels impossible to reason about, though.
turns.zh: 我一直不知道该怎么给它一个数字。我读过的每一个解释为什么它概率极高的论点，在我看来都很有道理。但感觉无法推理。

turns.en: What about you? Same thing. I go maybe, but it's still what it is. And I have to—the main thing is I think it doesn't matter. I think it's like, okay, it kind of—it definitely matters, I guess, but there's no way to know currently. And also, what matters though? If this really is—I mean, our inherent understanding of life is that we are these biological creatures that interact with other biological creatures, we mate and breed, and that this creates more of us, and then hopefully as society advances and we acquire more information, more understanding and knowledge, this next version of society will be superior to the version that preceded it. That's just how we look at society today. Nobody wants to live in 1860 where you died of a cold and there's no cure for infections. It's much better to be alive now, inarguably, right? Unless you really do prefer the simple life that you see on Yellowstone or something. What we're dealing with now in terms of access to information, the lack of ignorance if you choose to seek out information—you have so much more access to it now than ever before. That's so cool. And over time, if you go back to the beginning of written history to now, one of the things that is clearly evident is the more access to information, the better choices people can make. They don't always make better choices, but they certainly have much more potential to make better choices with more access to information. You know, we think that this is just this biological thing, but imagine if that's not what's going on. Imagine if this is a program and that you are just consciousness that's connected to this thing that's creating this experience that is indistinguishable from what we like to think of as a real biological experience from carbon-based life forms interacting with solid physical things in the real world. It's still unclear to me what I'm supposed to do differently or think.
turns.zh: 你呢？我也是。我可能会说也许，但它还是那样。而且我必须——主要是我觉得这没关系。我觉得，好吧，它确实有关系，我猜，但目前没办法知道。而且，什么才重要呢？如果这真的是——我的意思是，我们对生命固有的理解是，我们是这些生物体，与其他生物体互动，我们交配繁殖，这产生了更多的我们，然后随着社会进步，我们获得更多信息、更多理解和知识，下一代社会将比前一代更优越。这就是我们今天看待社会的方式。没有人想活在1860年，那时你会因为感冒而死，感染无药可治。现在活着要好得多，无可争议，对吧？除非你真的更喜欢《黄石》里那种简单生活。我们现在拥有的信息获取渠道，如果你选择去获取信息，你比以往任何时候都有更多的途径。这太酷了。而且随着时间的推移，如果你从有文字记载的历史开始到现在，一个明显的事实是，信息获取越多，人们就能做出更好的选择。他们并不总是做出更好的选择，但有了更多信息，他们肯定有更大的潜力做出更好的选择。你知道，我们认为这只是一个生物学现象，但想象一下如果并非如此。想象一下这是一个程序，你只是连接到这个程序的意识，它创造了这种体验，这种体验与我们通常认为的、碳基生命与真实世界中固体物理事物互动的真实生物体验无法区分。我仍然不清楚我应该做什么不同的事，或者想什么。

turns.en: Yeah, there's no answer. You're 100% right. What can you do differently? I mean, if you exist as if it's a simulation, if you just live your life as if it's a simulation, is that the solution? I don't know. I think it's real to me no matter what. It's real. I'm going to live it that way. And that will be the problem with an actual simulation if and when it does get implemented. Yeah, if we do create an actual simulation that's...
turns.zh: 是的，没有答案。你百分之百正确。你能做什么不同的事呢？我的意思是，如果你像对待模拟一样存在，如果你只是像活在模拟中一样生活，那是解决方案吗？我不知道。我觉得无论如何它对我来说是真实的。它是真实的。我会那样生活。而如果真正的模拟真的实现了，那就会成为问题。是的，如果我们真的创造了一个真实的模拟……

turns.en: Indistinguishable from real life. Like, what are the rules of the simulation? How does it work? Is that simulation fair and equitable, much more reasonable and peaceful? Is there no war in that simulation? Should we all agree to hook up to it because we will have a completely different experience in life? All the angst of crime and violence, the things we truly are terrified of, will be non-existent in this simulation.
turns.zh: 与现实生活无法区分。比如，模拟的规则是什么？它如何运作？那个模拟是否公平公正、更加合理和平？模拟中没有战争吗？我们是否都应该同意接入它，因为我们将拥有完全不同的生活体验？所有对犯罪和暴力的焦虑，那些我们真正恐惧的东西，在这个模拟中都将不复存在。

turns.en: Yeah, I mean, if we keep going, it seems like if you just extrapolate from where VR is now... Did you see the podcast that Lex Fridman did with Mark Zuckerberg? I saw some clips but haven't watched the whole thing yet. Right, so they're essentially using very realistic physical avatars in the metaverse. That's step one, maybe step three. Maybe it's a little beyond Pong at that point. Yeah, maybe it's Atari, maybe you're playing Space Invaders now. But whatever it is, it's on the path to this thing that will be indistinguishable. That seems inevitable. Those two things seem inevitable to me.
turns.zh: 是的，我的意思是，如果我们继续下去，似乎只要从现在的VR外推……你看过Lex Fridman和马克·扎克伯格做的播客吗？我看了一些片段，但还没看完整。对，所以他们基本上是在元宇宙中使用非常逼真的物理化身。那是第一步，也许是第三步。在那个时候可能比Pong稍微高级一点。是的，也许是雅达利，也许你现在在玩太空侵略者。但不管是什么，它都走在通往那个无法区分的事物的道路上。这似乎是不可避免的。这两件事对我来说似乎是不可避免的。

turns.en: The inevitable thing to me is that we will create a life form that is an artificial intelligent life form that's far more advanced than us. And once it becomes sentient, it will be able to create a far better version of itself. And then as it has better versions of itself, it will keep going. And if it keeps going, it will reach Godlike capabilities: the complete understanding of every aspect of the universe and the structure of itself, how to manipulate it, how to travel through it, how to communicate. And that, you know, if we keep going, if we survive a hundred years, a thousand years, ten thousand years, and we're still on this same technological exponential increasing in capability path, that's God. We become something like a God. And that might be what we do. That might be what intelligent, curious, innovative life actually does: it creates something that creates the very universe that we live in, like creates the next simulation. And then yeah, maybe that's the birth of the universe itself: creativity and intelligence, and that it all comes from that.
turns.zh: 对我来说不可避免的是，我们将创造一种生命形式，一种远比我们先进的人工智能生命形式。一旦它变得有知觉，它就能创造出比自己好得多的版本。然后随着它拥有更好的版本，它会继续前进。如果它继续下去，它将达到神一般的能力：完全理解宇宙的每一个方面及其自身的结构，如何操纵它，如何穿越它，如何交流。而且，你知道，如果我们继续下去，如果我们存活一百年、一千年、一万年，并且我们仍然在这条技术能力指数级增长的道路上，那就是神。我们变成了类似神的东西。那可能就是我们所做的。那可能就是智能、好奇、创新的生命真正做的事情：它创造了一个东西，这个东西又创造了我们所居住的宇宙，就像创造了下一个模拟。然后是的，也许那就是宇宙本身的诞生：创造力和智能，一切都源于此。

turns.en: I used to have this joke about the Big Bang: what if the Big Bang is just a natural thing? Like humans get so advanced that they create a Big Bang machine. And then, you know, we're so autistic and riddled with Adderall that we have no concept or worry of the consequences. And someone's like, 'I'll press it,' and they press it, and boom, we start from scratch every 14 billion years. And then that's what a Big Bang is. I mean, I don't know where it goes, but I do know that if you looked at the human race from afar, if you were an alien life form completely detached from any understanding of our culture, any understanding of our biological imperatives, and you just looked at what is this one dominant species doing on this planet: it makes better things. That's what it does.
turns.zh: 我以前有个关于大爆炸的笑话：如果大爆炸只是一件自然的事情呢？比如人类变得如此先进，以至于他们创造了一台大爆炸机器。然后，你知道，我们如此自闭，又充满了阿德拉，以至于我们对后果毫无概念或担忧。然后有人说‘我来按’，他们按下去，砰，我们每140亿年从头开始。那就是大爆炸。我的意思是，我不知道它会走向何方，但我确实知道，如果你从远处观察人类，如果你是一个外星生命形式，完全脱离对我们文化的任何理解，对我们生物本能的任何理解，你只是看着这个占主导地位的物种在这个星球上做什么：它制造更好的东西。这就是它所做的。

turns.en: I agree. It goes to war, it steals, it does a bunch of things it shouldn't do, it pollutes, it does all these terrible things. But it also consistently and constantly creates better things. Whether it's better weapons, going from the catapult to the rifle to the cannonballs to the rocket ships to the hypersonic missiles to nuclear bombs, it creates better and better things. That's the number one thing it does. And it's never happy with what it has. And you add that to consumerism, which is baked into us, and this constant desire for new or better things. Well, that fuels that innovation because it gives it the resources it needs to consistently innovate and constantly create newer and better things.
turns.zh: 我同意。它发动战争，它偷窃，它做很多不该做的事，它污染，它做所有这些可怕的事情。但它也持续不断地创造更好的东西。无论是更好的武器，从投石机到步枪到炮弹到火箭船到高超音速导弹到核弹，它创造越来越好的东西。这是它做的第一要务。而且它从不满足于已有的东西。再加上消费主义，这已经融入我们的骨子里，这种对新事物或更好事物的持续渴望。嗯，这推动了创新，因为它提供了持续创新和不断创造更新更好事物所需的资源。

turns.en: Well, if I was an alien life form, I'd be like, 'Oh, what is it doing? It's trying to create better.' Well, what is the forefront of it? Technology. Technology is the most transformative, the most spectacular, the most interesting thing that we create. And the most alien thing: the fact that we are so comfortable that you can FaceTime with someone in New Zealand instantly. We can get used to anything pretty quickly, you know, and take it for granted almost.
turns.zh: 嗯，如果我是一个外星生命形式，我会想，‘哦，它在做什么？它在试图创造更好的东西。’那么它的前沿是什么？技术。技术是我们创造的最具变革性、最壮观、最有趣的东西。而且最奇怪的是：我们如此习以为常，以至于你可以立即与新西兰的人FaceTime。我们很快就能习惯任何事情，你知道，几乎把它视为理所当然。

turns.en: Yeah. And well, if you were an alien life form and you were looking at us, you're like, 'What is it doing? Oh, it keeps making better things. It's going to keep making better things.' Well, if it keeps making better things, it's going to make a better version of a thinking thing. And it's doing that right now, and you're a part of that. It's going to make a better version of a thinking thing. Well, that better version of a thinking thing is basically now in the amoeba stage, it's in the small multicellular life form stage. Well, what if that version becomes an Oppenheimer? What if that version becomes, like, if it scales up so far that it becomes so hyper-intelligent that it is completely alien to any other intelligent life form that has ever existed here before? And it constantly does the same thing: makes better and better versions of itself. Well, where does that go? It goes to a god, it goes to something like a God. And maybe God is a real thing, but maybe it's a real consequence of this process that human beings have of consistently, constantly innovating and constantly having this desire to push the envelope of creativity and technological power.
turns.zh: 是的。嗯，如果你是一个外星生命形式，看着我们，你会想，‘它在做什么？哦，它一直在制造更好的东西。它会继续制造更好的东西。’嗯，如果它继续制造更好的东西，它就会制造出更好的思考的东西。它现在就在这样做，而你是其中的一部分。它会制造出更好的思考的东西。嗯，那个更好的思考的东西现在基本上处于阿米巴原虫阶段，处于小型多细胞生命形式阶段。嗯，如果那个版本变成了奥本海默呢？如果那个版本变得，比如，如果它扩展得如此之大，变得如此超级智能，以至于与之前存在过的任何其他智能生命形式完全不同呢？而且它不断做同样的事情：制造越来越好的自身版本。嗯，那会走向何方？它会走向神，走向类似神的东西。也许神是真实存在的，但也许它是人类持续不断创新、不断渴望突破创造力和技术能力界限这一过程的真实结果。

turns.en: I guess it comes down to maybe a definitional disagreement about what you mean by 'it becomes a god.' Like, I can totally... I think it becomes something much, unbelievably much smarter and more capable than we are. And what does that thing become if that keeps going? And maybe the way you mean it as a Godlike force is that that thing can then go create, can go simulate a universe. Yes, okay, that I can resonate with. I think whatever we create will still be subject to the laws of physics in this universe, right?
turns.zh: 我想这归结为也许在定义上存在分歧，关于你说的‘它变成神’是什么意思。比如，我完全可以……我认为它变得比我们聪明得多、能力强得多。如果继续下去，那个东西会变成什么？也许你所说的神一般的力量是指那个东西可以创造、可以模拟一个宇宙。是的，好吧，这个我能共鸣。我认为我们创造的任何东西仍然要受这个宇宙物理定律的约束，对吧？

turns.en: Yeah, maybe that is the overlying fabric that God exists in. The 'god' word is a loaded word because it's just been so co-opted. But you know, I was having this conversation with Stephen Meyer, who is a physicist, I believe he's a physicist. He's a physicist and he's also religious. It was a really weird conversation, very fascinating conversation. Believer in Christ, yeah. He even believes in the resurrection, which I found very interesting. But it's interesting communicating with him because he has these little pre-designed speeches. He's encountered all these questions so many times that he has these very well-worded, very articulate responses to these things that I sense are like bits, you know? Like when I'm talking to a comic, a comic has this bit on train travel and they tell you the bit. That's what it's like. He has bits on why he believes in Jesus and why he believes. Very, very intelligent guy. But I propose the question: when we're thinking about God, what if instead of God created the universe, what if the universe is God? And the creative...
turns.zh: 是的，也许那就是神存在的上层结构。‘神’这个词是个有争议的词，因为它已经被过度利用了。但你知道，我和斯蒂芬·迈耶有过一次对话，他是一位物理学家，我相信他是物理学家。他既是物理学家，也是宗教信徒。那是一次非常奇怪的对话，非常迷人的对话。他信仰基督，是的。他甚至相信复活，我觉得这很有趣。但和他交流很有趣，因为他有这些预先设计好的小演讲。他遇到过这些问题太多次了，以至于他对这些事情有非常措辞优美、非常清晰的回答，我感觉到这些就像段子，你知道吗？就像我和一个喜剧演员聊天时，喜剧演员有一个关于火车旅行的段子，然后他告诉你那个段子。就是那样。他有关于为什么他相信耶稣、为什么他相信的段子。非常非常聪明的人。但我提出这个问题：当我们思考神时，如果不是神创造了宇宙，而是宇宙就是神呢？而创造性的……

transcript.secZh: 不可避免的AI与神级能力

turns.en: The force of all life and everything is the universe itself, instead of thinking there's this thing that created us. This is close to a lot of Eastern religions. I think this is an easier thing to wrap my mind around than many other religions. When I do psychedelics, I get that feeling like there's this insane suit of innovation and connectivity all around us, but our minds are so primal. We're this thing—this is what we used to be. And what is that? There's a guy named Shane Against the Machine, an artist who created a chimpanzee skull made out of Zilant symbols. It's got a little Zilant on the back. He just made this dope art piece. Cool. But I wonder if our limitations are that we are an advanced version of primates. We still have all these things: jealousy, envy, anxiety, lust, anger, fear, violence—all these detrimental traits that were important for us to survive and get to this point. As time goes on, we will figure out a way to engineer those out. As intelligent life becomes more intelligent, and we create a version of intelligent life far more capable than us, if that keeps going—I mean, ChatGPT. Imagine going back to Socrates and showing him that, explaining it, showing him a phone. He'd be like, 'What have you done? What is this?' I bet he'd be much more impressed with the phone than ChatGPT. The phone's ability to communicate, but then the access to information would be so profound. Back then, Galileo was put under house arrest for saying the Earth is not the center of the universe. Now we know it's not. We have satellites, we send cameras into orbit.
turns.zh: 所有生命和万物的力量就是宇宙本身，而不是认为有一个创造我们的东西。这接近很多东方宗教。我觉得这比许多其他宗教更容易理解。当我服用迷幻药时，我有那种感觉：周围存在着一种疯狂的创新和连接，但我们的思想太原始了。我们就是这种东西——这是我们曾经的样子。那是什么？有一个叫Shane Against the Machine的艺术家，他用Zilant符号做了一个黑猩猩头骨。背面有一个小Zilant。他做了这件很酷的艺术品。但我在想，我们的局限性是否在于我们是灵长类动物的高级版本。我们仍然有所有这些：嫉妒、羡慕、焦虑、欲望、愤怒、恐惧、暴力——所有这些曾经对我们生存和走到这一步很重要的有害特质。随着时间的推移，我们会想办法通过工程手段消除它们。随着智能生命变得更加智能，我们创造出比我们更强大的智能生命版本，如果这种情况持续下去——我是说ChatGPT。想象一下回到苏格拉底时代，给他看那个，解释它，给他看一部手机。他会说，‘你做了什么？这是什么？’我打赌他会对手机比ChatGPT更印象深刻。手机的通信能力，但获取信息的方式会如此深刻。那时，伽利略因为说地球不是宇宙的中心而被软禁。现在我们知道它不是。我们有卫星，我们把相机送入轨道。

turns.en: I totally get that. We kind of know what it's like to talk to a smart person, so you're like, 'Oh, I didn't think you could talk to a non-person and have them be person-like in some responses some of the time.' But a phone—if you just woke up after 2,000 years and there was a phone, you'd have no model for that, and you didn't get to get there gradually.
turns.zh: 我完全理解。我们大致知道和聪明人说话是什么感觉，所以你会想，‘哦，我没想到你可以和一个非人说话，而他们有时在回应中表现得像人。’但手机——如果你在2000年后醒来，看到一部手机，你对此没有任何模型，而且你没有逐渐到达那里。

turns.en: Yeah, you didn't. My friend Eddie Griffin has a joke about how Alexander Graham Bell must have been doing coke, because only someone on coke could be like, 'I want to talk to someone who's not even here.' That's what a phone is. Does coke make people want to do that? I don't know, I've never done coke, but I imagine it does. It seems to me like it just makes people angry and chaotic.
turns.zh: 是的，你没有。我的朋友Eddie Griffin有个笑话，说亚历山大·格拉汉姆·贝尔一定是在吸可卡因，因为只有吸了可卡因的人才会说，‘我想和一个不在这里的人说话。’这就是电话。可卡因会让人想那样做吗？我不知道，我没吸过，但我想是的。在我看来，它只会让人愤怒和混乱。

turns.en: Yeah, a little, but they also have ideas.
turns.zh: 是的，有一点，但他们也有想法。

turns.en: Back to this: where does it go if it keeps going? It has to go to some impossible level of capability. Just think of what we can do now with nuclear power, nuclear bombs, hypersonic missiles—the insane physical things we've taken out of human creativity and imagination, through engineering and technology, implemented these physical devices that are indistinguishable from magic if you brought them 500 years ago. I think it's quite remarkable. So keep going 100,000 years from now, if we're still here, if something like us is still here, what can it do? In the same way that I don't think Socrates would have predicted the phone, I can't predict that. I'm probably totally off. But maybe that's also why comets exist—maybe it's a nice reset. Just leave a few around, give them a distant memory of the utopian world that used to exist, have them go through thousands of years of barbarism and horrific behavior, and then reestablish society. This is the Younger Dryas impact theory: around 11,800 years ago, at the end of the Ice Age, we were hit by multiple comets that caused instantaneous melting of the ice caps over North America, everything flooded—the source of flood myths from the Epic of Gilgamesh and the Bible. There's physical evidence: core samples show high levels of iridium, common in space but rare on Earth, and micro diamonds from impacts. About 30% of the Earth has evidence of this. It's very likely that proponents of this theory are correct, and that's why they find ancient structures dating to 11,000-12,000 years ago, when people were thought to be hunter-gatherers. Maybe our timeline is really off.
turns.zh: 回到这个问题：如果继续下去，它会走向何方？它必须达到某种不可能的能力水平。想想我们现在能用核能、核弹、高超音速导弹做什么——那些从人类创造力和想象力中提取出来的疯狂物理事物，通过工程和技术实现，这些物理设备如果带到500年前，将和魔法无异。我认为这非常了不起。所以继续下去，10万年后，如果我们还在，如果像我们这样的东西还在，它能做什么？就像我不认为苏格拉底能预测手机一样，我无法预测。我可能完全错了。但也许这就是彗星存在的原因——也许是一次很好的重置。留下一些，给他们一个曾经存在的乌托邦世界的遥远记忆，让他们经历数千年的野蛮和恐怖行为，然后重建社会。这就是新仙女木期撞击理论：大约11800年前，在冰河时代末期，我们被多颗彗星击中，导致北美冰盖瞬间融化，一切都被淹没——这是《吉尔伽美什史诗》和《圣经》中洪水神话的来源。有物理证据：岩芯样本显示高水平的铱，这在太空中很常见，在地球上很稀有，还有撞击产生的微钻石。大约30%的地球有这方面的证据。这些理论的支持者很可能是正确的，这就是为什么他们发现可追溯到11000-12000年前的古代建筑，而当时人们被认为是狩猎采集者。也许我们的时间线完全错了。

turns.en: I've been watching that with interest. Randle Carlson is the greatest guy to pay attention to that. He's kind of dedicated his whole life to it, which by the way happened because of a psychedelic experience. He was on acid once, looking at this immense canyon, and he had this vision that it was created by instantaneous erosion of the polar caps, and that it just washed this wave of impossible water through the Earth, cutting these paths. Now there seems to be actual physical evidence that that probably took place. We're just the survivors, and we have reemerged. Society and human civilization occasionally gets set back to a primal place.
turns.zh: 我一直很感兴趣地关注着。Randle Carlson是最值得关注的人。他几乎一生都致力于此，顺便说一句，这源于一次迷幻体验。他有一次吸了酸，看着一个巨大的峡谷，他有一个幻象，认为它是由于极地冰盖瞬间侵蚀而形成的，一股不可思议的水浪冲过地球，切出了这些路径。现在似乎有实际的物理证据表明那可能发生过。我们只是幸存者，我们重新出现了。社会和人类文明偶尔会倒退到原始状态。

turns.en: Yeah, who knows. If you're right that what happens here is we kind of edit out all the impulses in ourselves that we don't like, we get to that world—seems kind of boring. So maybe that's when we have to make a new simulation to watch people think they're going through some drama. Or maybe we get to this point where we have this power, but the haves and the have-nots, the divide is too great, and people get a hold of this technology and use it to oppress people who don't have it. We didn't mitigate the human biological problems, the reward systems that we have—'I got to have more and you got to have less.' That's this natural inclination for competition, and someone hijacks that. I think this is going to be such a hugely important issue to get ahead of before the first people push that button. What do you think about when Elon was calling for a pause on AI? He was starting an AGI company while he was doing that.
